questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct with reference to the Constituent Assembly of India?
I. - Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha was chosen as the first temporary President, as he was the oldest member of the Assembly.
II. 	 The Assembly had two Vice-Presidents, H.C.
Mukherjee and G.V. Mavlankar.
III. It functioned as the legislative body of free India
until the first general elections in 1952.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constituent Assembly** **of India was set up in December 1946 under the scheme formulated by the Cabinet** **Mission Plan to frame the Constitution of independent India.** It performed a dual role – drafting the Constitution and functioning as the legislative body of free India until the first general elections in 1952.  **So, statement III is** **correct.**
● The first temporary President was **Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha**, the oldest member of the Assembly, following French practice. Later, **Dr. Rajendra Prasad** was elected as the permanent President. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Assembly had **two Vice-Presidents**: **H.C. Mukherjee** and **V.T. Krishnamachari**. They assisted the President in conducting the proceedings of the Assembly. This unique arrangement reflected the inclusive character and diversity of the Assembly. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The Constituent Assembly had to perform two distinct roles. When meeting as the Constituent Body, it was chaired by **Dr. Rajendra Prasad.** When meeting as the Legislative Body (Dominion Legislature), it was chaired by **G.V.** **Mavlankar.** Thus, effectively, two different Chairmen presided depending on the function being performed. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
In the context of the Constitution of India, the principle of substantive equality implies which of the following?
I. - Treating every individual in the same manner, irrespective of their social and economic conditions
II. 	 Taking positive measures by the State to reduce
existing inequalities among different sections of society
III. 	 Guaranteeing equality before the law under
Article 14 fully realizes substantive equality without the need for any special provisions.
IV. 	 Providing special provisions or affirmative
action to disadvantaged groups to achieve real equality of opportunity
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Substantive equality** embodies the constitutional principle that the law must **recognise and respond** to the **diverse** **social contexts, structural disadvantages, and historical injustices** experienced by individuals and groups. It transcends the notion of mere **formal equality —** which insists on identical treatment — by emphasising the equitable outcomes and actual impacts of laws and policies**.** Whereas formal equality is concerned primarily with procedural uniformity and the consistent application of rules, substantive equality is grounded in the ethical imperative of social justice and seeks redistribution of opportunities and resources to achieve genuine parity of status and opportunity among citizens.
● **Treating everyone the same, irrespective of their background, reflects formal equality,** not substantive equality. **Substantive equality recognises that identical treatment can perpetuate inequality if individuals** **start from unequal positions. So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Substantive equality** **requires the State to take positive action** to address systemic disadvantages.  The Supreme Court in **State of Punjab v. Davinder Singh (2024)** reaffirmed that the State can adopt targeted measures (like sub-classification among SCs/STs) to ensure benefits reach the most disadvantaged. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Equality before law under Article 14 represents formal equality** (a British concept), ensuring equal treatment in law. However, **substantive equality requires special provisions** under Articles 15(4), 15(5), and 16(4). Article 14 alone cannot deliver substantive equality without these enabling clauses. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● Providing **special provisions or affirmative action** (reservations, welfare schemes, targeted benefits) is a constitutional expression of substantive equality. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
With respect to the Fundamental Rights of the Armed Forces, consider the following statements:
I. - The President of India, as the Supreme Commander, is empowered to restrict or suspend the Fundamental Rights of the Armed Forces.
II. 	 Court-martial proceedings under military laws
can be excluded from the writ jurisdiction of the Supreme Court and High Courts.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constitution of India** recognises the need for maintaining **discipline and efficiency in the Armed Forces**, police, and allied forces, even if it necessitates **restricting certain Fundamental Rights.** This is constitutionally provided under **Article 33** which empowers the Parliament to make such laws.
● **Article 33** explicitly **empowers only Parliament**, **not the President or the Executive,** to modify or abrogate the application of **Fundamental Rights** in respect of:
o Members of the Armed Forces, o Members of para-military or police forces, o Members of intelligence agencies, and o Persons employed in analogous services. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Any **law made by Parliament under Article 33** for restricting or abrogating Fundamental Rights **cannot be** **challenged in any court** on the ground that it **contravenes the Fundamental Rights** guaranteed by Part III of the Constitution.  This immunity ensures the **autonomy and operational integrity** of the Armed Forces and associated services.
● As per **the R.S. Bhagat v. Union of India case,** Court Martial proceedings are subject to the writ jurisdiction of both the Supreme Court and the High Courts. Specifically, the Supreme Court can issue a writ of certiorari under Article 32, while High Courts can do so under Article 226 of the Constitution. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
Consider the following functionaries:
I. 	 Members of the State Council of Ministers
II. 	 Members of the State Public Service
Commission
III. 	Advocate General of the State
IV. 	 State Election Commissioner
V. 	 District Judge
How many of the above-mentioned functionaries are appointed by the Governor and are also subject to removal by him?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Article 164 of the Constitution of India says that the **Chief Minister shall be appointed by the Governor and the** **other Ministers shall be appointed by the Governor** on the advice of the Chief Minister. It also says that the **Ministers shall hold office during the pleasure of the Governor.** They can be removed by the Governor on the advice of the Chief Minister. **So, point I is correct.**
● **The Chairman and other members of the State Public Service Commission (SPSC) are appointed by the** **Governor** of the state. Although the chairman and members of an SPSC are appointed by the Governor, **they can** **be removed only by the President (and not by the Governor)**. The President can remove them on the same grounds and in the same manner as he can remove a chairman or a member of the UPSC**. So, point II is not correct.**
● According to Article 165 of the Constitution, the **Advocate-General for the State is appointed by the Governor.** **It says that “**The Governor of each State shall appoint a person who is qualified to be appointed a Judge of a High Court to be Advocate-General for the State.” It further says that “The **Advocate-General shall hold office during** **the pleasure of the Governor**”. Thus, he can be removed by the Governor.  **So, point III is correct.**
● Article 243K of the Constitution says that the **State Election Commissioner is appointed by the Governor.** His conditions of service and tenure of office shall also be determined by the Governor. He shall not be removed from office except in the manner and on the grounds prescribed for the removal of the judge of the state high court. This means **he is removed by the President** on the recommendation of the Parliament. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **District Judges** in any State are **appointed by the Governor** of the State in consultation with the State High Court. **The Governor can remove the District Judge** after the confirmation of the High Court collegium. **So, point V is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
With reference to the State Legislative Assembly, consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Constitution of India has fixed both
the maximum and minimum strength of the Assembly.
II. 	 The Governor of a State can nominate one
member from the Anglo-Indian community to the Assembly.
III. 	Only four Union Territories in India possess
their own Legislative Assemblies.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Articles **168 to 212** in **Part VI** of the Constitution deal with the **organisation, composition, duration, and powers** of the **State Legislature**, which is broadly similar to the **Parliament**, though with some differences.
● The Legislative Assembly consists of representatives directly elected by the people based on universal adult franchise. Its **maximum strength** is fixed at **500** and **minimum strength** at **60**, meaning the **size of the Legislative** **Assembly** varies from **60 to 500** depending on the **population of the state**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● However, in the case of Arunachal Pradesh, Sikkim, and Goa, the minimum number is fixed at 30, while in Mizoram and Nagaland, it is 40 and 46, respectively.
● Before **2020**, Governors nominated one member from the **Anglo-Indian community** to the Assembly if not adequately represented. This provision **ceased on 25 January 2020** with the **104th Constitutional Amendment** **Act, 2019**, discontinuing such nominations.  **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Only three (Delhi, Puducherry, and Jammu & Kashmir) out of the eight Union Territories** **have Legislative** **Assemblies.** The remaining five are directly administered by the **Central Government**. **So, statement III is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About State Legislative Assembly:**
● Like the **Lok Sabha**, the **Legislative Assembly** is **not a continuing chamber**. Its **normal term** is **five years** from the date of its **first meeting after the general elections**. The expiry of this five-year period leads to the **automatic dissolution** of the Assembly.
● The **Governor** has the **authority to dissolve** the Assembly **at any time**, even before the completion of its term, to allow for **fresh elections**.
● During a period of **National Emergency**, the **term of the Legislative Assembly** can be **extended by Parliament** through a law for **one year at a time**, and such extensions can continue **as long as the emergency is in operation**.
● However, this **extension cannot continue beyond six months** after the **Emergency has ceased** **to operate**, meaning that the **Assembly must be re-elected** within **six months** after the revocation of the Emergency.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Central Council of Ministers, Cabinet Committees
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Cabinet Secretariat of India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It functions directly under the Union Home
Minister.
II. 	 It is the custodian of the papers of the Cabinet
meetings.
III. 	It is directly responsible for the implementation
of plans and programmes of each Ministry.
IV.	 All Ministries send a monthly report to the
Cabinet Secretariat providing details on important policy matters.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Cabinet Secretariat functions under** the **Prime Minister** (through the Cabinet Secretary), not under the Union Home Minister. It supports the Cabinet and the PM in decision-making and coordination.  **So, statement I** **is not correct.**
● The **Cabinet Secretary is the administrative head of the Cabinet Secretariat, not the Secretariat of** **Parliament**.  The **Secretariat of Parliament, however, is headed by the Secretary-General of Lok Sabha for** **the Lok Sabha and the Secretary-General of Rajya Sabha for the Rajya Sabha.** These are separate entities from the Cabinet Secretariat and are under the respective presiding officers (Speaker and Chairman).
● The Secretariat prepares and circulates the Cabinet agenda and papers, records discussions, and is the statutory **custodian of those papers**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Cabinet Secretariat** is responsible for the **administration of the Government of India (Allocation of** **Business) Rules, 1961 and the Government of India (Transaction of Business) Rules, 1961**. These rules govern the allocation and transaction of government business among ministries and departments.
● **The Secretariat monitors implementation** of Cabinet decisions and promotes inter-ministerial coordination, but **actual implementation** of policies, plans and programmes is the **responsibility of the respective** **Ministries/Departments. So, statement III is not correct.**
● **Ministries/Departments send regular updates** (monthly DO letters/ summaries) **to the Cabinet Secretariat** to keep the Cabinet Secretary apprised of important developments and policy matters. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Post Constitutional Requirement for Removal
I - President Two-thirds of the total membership of the House
II. - Vice President
Rajya Sabha passes a resolution with an effective majority and the Lok Sabha agrees to it by a simple majority
III. - Chief Justice of India
More than two-thirds of members present and voting and the majority of the total membership of the House
IV. - Comptroller and Auditor General of India
The majority of all the then members of the House
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Constitution of India prescribes different removal processes for different constitutional offices to maintain the balance of power, ensure independence, and protect the dignity and impartiality of each office based on its role and importance.
● **Article 67(b)** of the Constitution provides that the **Vice President can be removed if the Rajya Sabha passes a** **resolution with an effective majority, meaning a majority of all the then members of the House, and the Lok** **Sabha agrees to it by a simple majority. So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● Under **Article 61**, the **President can be removed through an impeachment process that requires a two-thirds** **majority of members present and voting in both Houses of Parliament.** This majority applies only to those present and voting, as long as the required quorum is maintained. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● Under **Article 124(4)**, the **Chief Justice of India can be removed in the same manner as any Supreme Court** **judge**. The process requires **each House of Parliament to pass a motion supported by a majority of its total** **membership and by two-thirds of the members present and voting.** **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● Under **Article 148(1),** the **Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) can be removed in the same manner as a** **Supreme Court judge**. This requires approval from each House of Parliament by a **majority of its total** **membership and a two-thirds majority of members present and voting,** making the removal process stricter than a simple majority. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
With reference to the appointment of Additional and Acting Judges of a High Court, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Additional judges serve until the backlog
is cleared, whereas acting judges may be appointed for up to two years.
II. 	 Neither the additional nor the acting judge
can hold office after attaining the age of 62 years.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **President** can appoint **duly qualified persons** as **Additional judges** of a **High Court** for a **temporary period** **not exceeding two years** when there is a **temporary increase in the business** of the High Court, or there are **arrears of work** in the High Court.
● On the other hand, the **President** can appoint a **duly qualified person** as an **Acting judge** of a High Court when a **judge of that High Court (other than the Chief Justice)** is **unable to perform the duties** of his/her office due to **absence or any other reason**; or **appointed to act temporarily as Chief Justice** of that High Court.
● An **Acting judge** holds office **until the permanent judge resumes** his/her duties. However, both the **Additional** and **Acting judges** **cannot hold office after attaining the age of 62 years**. **So, statement I is not correct and** **statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Other Judges:**
● **Acting Chief Justice:**  The **President** can appoint a **judge of a High Court** as an **Acting Chief Justice** of that High Court when:
o The office of the Chief Justice of the High Court is **vacant**; or o The Chief Justice of the High Court is **temporarily absent**; or o The Chief Justice of the High Court is **unable to perform the duties** of his/her office.
● **Retired Judges:** At any time, the **Chief Justice of a High Court** of a state can request a **retired judge** of that High Court or **any other High Court** to **act as a judge** of the High Court of that state for a **temporary** **period**. This can be done **only with the previous consent** of the **President** and also of the **person to be** **appointed**.
o Such a **judge** is entitled to **allowances** as the **President may determine** and will enjoy **all the** **jurisdiction, powers, and privileges** of a judge of that High Court. However, he/she will **not** **otherwise be deemed to be a judge** of that High Court.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding parliamentary offices in India:
I. 	 The office of the Whip is a statutory post.
II. 	 The Leader of the Opposition is a statutory
position and is entitled to salary, allowances, and facilities equivalent to those of a Cabinet Minister.
III. 	The position of the Leader of the House is
based on parliamentary convention.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A constitutional post refers to an office or position that is established and defined by the Constitution of India. These posts derive their authority, powers, functions, tenure, and method of appointment or removal directly from constitutional provisions, not from ordinary laws or executive orders. Holders of such posts perform vital functions to uphold the constitutional framework and maintain checks and balances in governance.
● The **Whip is appointed by political parties** to ensure party discipline in the legislature. The office is not mentioned in the Constitution. It is a **parliamentary convention**, not a constitutional or statutory creation. **So, statement I is** **not correct.**
● The **Leader of the Opposition (LoP)** is not a constitutional post but a **statutory one,** established under the **Salary** **and Allowances of Leaders of Opposition in Parliament Act, 1977.** The LoP must come from the largest opposition party in the House, which should have at least 10% of the total members. **The Speaker or Chairman** **recognizes the LoP**, who plays an important role in providing opposition to the government and has a **status** **equivalent to a Cabinet minister.** **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Leader of the House is a parliamentary convention**, not mentioned in the Constitution. In the **Lok Sabha,** this role is **usually held by the Prime Minister if they are a member; otherwise, a minister nominated by the** **Prime Minister** takes the position. In the **Rajya Sabha**, the **Leader of the House is a minister nominated by the** **Prime Minister.** The Leader is responsible for managing government business, organizing debates, and representing the government's position in the House. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Article 19(1)

options.a:
Of the Constitution of India:
I. 	 Speech with purely economic or commercial
intent is protected in the same manner as an individual's freedom of speech and expression.
II. 	 Decency or morality is explicitly recognised
as a constitutionally valid ground for limiting free speech and expression.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 19(1)(a)** of the Constitution of India guarantees to all citizens the **“freedom of speech and expression”**, forming one of the most vital pillars of Indian democracy. This right allows individuals to express **opinions, share** **information, and engage in dialogue through any medium**—oral, written, print, visual, or digital.
● During the hearing of a petition by Cure SMA Foundation of India against comedians like Samay Raina on August 25, 2025, the Supreme Court made the distinction that **commercial and prohibited speeches are not covered** **under the fundamental right to free speech.**
o **The court observed that the freedom of speech and expression guaranteed by Article 19(1)(a) of the** **Constitution does not apply to commercial speech in the same way. So, statement I is not correct.** o In **Telecom Watchdog v. Union of India (2012),** the **Delhi High Court** makes a distinction between “**purely** **commercial advertisement**,” the purpose of which is to further trade and commerce — thus
placing it **outside the concept of freedom of speech and expression**  and those types of commercial speech whose purpose is the propagation of ideas — social, political or economic, or ideas in furtherance of literature or human thought.” Such limitation on the scope of commercial speech can be noted from A. Suresh v. State of Tamil Nadu (1997), where the Supreme Court held that in cases where “freedom of speech gets intertwined with business it undergoes a fundamental change and its exercise has to be balanced against societal interest.
● **Article 19(2)** permits the State to impose **reasonable restrictions** on the exercise of free speech on specific grounds, namely:
o Sovereignty and integrity of India o Security of the State o Friendly relations with foreign states o Public order o **Decency or morality** o Contempt of court o Defamation o Incitement to an offence
● The Supreme Court has consistently held that restrictions must be **reasonable, proportionate, and directly** **connected** to these grounds. Therefore, **decency or morality is explicitly recognised** as a constitutionally valid ground for limiting free speech. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Supreme Court has recently directed the Centre to frame guidelines for regulating social media, stressing that free speech cannot be misused for commercial gain at the cost of public dignity.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Supreme Court and the High
Courts in India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 The power to expand the jurisdiction of High
Courts rests exclusively with Parliament.
II. 	 The pension payable to the Judges of High
Courts is charged upon the Consolidated Fund of India.
III. 	Both the Supreme Court and the High Courts
possess the power to punish for contempt of court.
IV. 	 The Constitution expressly empowers both
the Supreme Court and the High Courts to review their own judgments.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Articles 214 to 231 in Part VI of the Constitution of India** deal with the provisions related to the High Courts.
● The Constitution does not detail the jurisdiction and powers of High Courts; it largely preserves what existed before its commencement, with additions such as revenue jurisdiction, writ jurisdiction, superintendence, and consultative powers. While **Parliament and state legislatures can modify High Court jurisdiction,** the powers expressly granted by the Constitution itself cannot be curtailed by either.  **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The salaries, allowances, and **pensions of the judges and the staff**, as well as all the administrative expenses **of** **the Supreme Court**, are **charged on the Consolidated Fund of India**. Thus, they are non-votable by the Parliament (though they can be discussed by it).
o The salaries and allowances of the judges, the **salaries, allowances, and pensions of the staff**, as well as the administrative expenses of a High Court, are **charged on the Consolidated Fund of the State**. Thus, they are non-votable by the State Legislature (though they can be discussed by it). It should be noted that the **pension of a High Court judge** is **charged on the Consolidated Fund of India** and not the State. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The **Supreme Court** can **punish any person for its contempt**. Thus, its **actions and decisions** cannot be **criticised** **or opposed** by anybody. This power is vested in the Supreme Court to **maintain its authority, dignity, and** **honour**.
o A High Court also has the **power to punish for contempt of itself** under the **Contempt of Courts Act,** **1971**, which classifies contempt into **civil** and **criminal** types. The High Court can also **punish for** **contempt of subordinate courts**, except when the alleged contempt is already an **offence under the** **Indian Penal Code (1860)**. Thus, its **actions and decisions** cannot be **criticised or opposed** by anybody. This power is vested in the High Court to **maintain its authority, dignity, and honour**. **So, statement III** **is correct.**
● As a **Court of Record**, the High Court possesses the **inherent power to review, correct, or modify its own** **judgment, order, or decision**, even though the **Constitution does not explicitly confer this power**. This means
that the High Court can revisit its earlier decisions to correct **errors apparent on the face of the record** or to **prevent miscarriage of justice**.
o In contrast, the **Supreme Court** has been **explicitly conferred** the **power of review** by the **Constitution** **of India** (under **Article 137**), whereas the High Court’s review power is **implied from its status** as a **Court** **of Record** under **Article 215**. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following expenditure:
I. 	 Expenditure on infrastructure projects
II. 	 Debt charges for which the Government of
India is liable
III. 	Salary and allowances of the Chairman and
Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha
IV. 	 Statutory grants made by the Union to the
States under Article 275
Which of the above are under the category of charged expenditure on the Consolidated Fund of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Charged expenditure (Article 112 (3)) refers to expenses that are not subject to vote in the Lok Sabha,** though Parliament can discuss them. These are automatically debited from the Consolidated Fund of India and include certain constitutional and statutory payments.
● **Expenditure on infrastructure projects, such as roads, bridges, and railways, is classified as non-charged** **expenditure**. These are developmental and plan expenditures aimed at building and improving physical infrastructure. Since they involve government development initiatives, such expenditures are votable, meaning they require approval by the Lok Sabha. **So, point I is not correct.**
● **Debt charges for which the Government of India is liable, including interest payments, repayment of** **principal, and other debt-related obligations**, are classified as **charged expenditure** under **Article 112(3)** of the Constitution. These are mandated payments that must be met from the Consolidated Fund of India and do not require approval by the Lok Sabha. **So, point II is correct.**
● **The Chairman and Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha** are constitutional posts under Articles 64 and 89. Their remuneration is **charged on the Consolidated Fund of India,** making it a charged expenditure. **So, point** **III is correct.**
● **Article 275 empowers the Parliament to make grants to the States that are in need of financial assistance and** **not to every State.** Also, different sums may be fixed for different States**. These sums are charged on the** **Consolidated Fund of India every year. So, point IV is correct.**
o Apart from this general provision, the Constitution also provides for specific grants for promoting the welfare of the scheduled tribes in a state or for raising the level of administration of the scheduled areas in a State, including the State of Assam. o The statutory grants under Article 275 (both general and specific) are given to the states on the recommendation of the Finance Commission. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following Department-related Standing Committees:
I. 	 Committee of Home Affairs
II. 	 Committee of Defence
III. 	Committee of External Affairs
IV.	 Committee on Personnel, Public Grievances,
Law and Justice
Which of the above committees work under the Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● On the recommendation of the **Rules Committee of the Lok Sabha**, **17 Departmentally-Related Standing** **Committees** (DRSCs) were **set up** in the Parliament in **1993**. In **2004, seven more** such committees were set up, thus increasing their number from **17 to 24.**
● The main objective of the standing committees is to **secure more accountability** of the Executive (i.e., the Council of Ministers) to the Parliament, particularly financial accountability.
● They also assist the Parliament in debating the budget more effectively. Each standing committee consists of **31** **members** **(21 from the Lok Sabha** and **10 from the Rajya Sabha**). The **members of the Lok Sabha** are **nominated by the Speaker** from amongst its own members, just as the **members of the Rajya Sabha** are **nominated** by the **Chairman** from amongst its members.
● Out of the 24 standing committees, **8 work under** the **Rajya Sabha** and **16** under the **Lok Sabha.**
● **Committees under the Rajya Sabha:**
o Committee on Commerce o **Committee on Home Affairs. So, point I is correct.** o Committee on Human Resource Development o Committee on Industry o Committee on Science & Technology, Environment & Forests
o Committee on Transport, Tourism and Culture o Committee on Health & Family Welfare o **Committee on Personnel, Public Grievances, Law and Justice. So, point IV is correct.**
● **Committees under the Lok Sabha**
o Committee on Agriculture o Committee on Information Technology o **Committee on Defence. So, point II is not correct.** o Committee on Energy o **Committee on External Affairs. So, point III is not correct.** o Committee on Finance o Committee on Food, Consumer Affairs and Public Distribution o Committee on Labour o Committee on Petroleum & Natural Gas o Committee on Railways o Committee on Urban Development o Committee on Water Resources o Committee on Chemicals and Fertilizers o Committee on Rural Development o Committee on Coal and Steel o Committee on Social Justice and Empowerment **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
With reference to the nature and enforceability
of Fundamental Rights under the Constitution of India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 All Fundamental Rights are self-executory in
nature.
II. 	 All Fundamental Rights are enforceable
against both the State and private individuals.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Fundamental Rights** enshrined in **Part III of the Constitution of India** guarantee essential freedoms and protections to individuals. However, their **nature and enforceability vary based on their scope** and the manner of implementation.
● **Not all Fundamental Rights are self-executory.** While most rights are directly enforceable (for example, rights under Articles 14, 19, 21, etc.), a few require enabling legislation to become operational. **Article 35 specifically** **empowers only the Parliament** (not State Legislatures) to make laws for giving effect to certain Fundamental Rights—such as those related to preventive detention (Article 22) and protection of rights of members of armed forces (Article 33). This ensures uniformity throughout the country. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Fundamental Rights** are **primarily** available against the **arbitrary action of the State, as defined in Article 12**. However, **some rights—**such as protection against untouchability **(Article 17)** and prohibition of human trafficking and forced labour **(Article 23)—**are also enforceable against **private individuals**. Therefore, it is incorrect to say that all Fundamental Rights are enforceable against both the state and private individuals. **So, statement II is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
questions.questionText:
Which of the following subjects comes under the
State List (List II) of the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India?
I. - Prisons
II. 	 Banking
III. 	Education
IV. 	 Agriculture
V. 	 Betting and Gambling
VI. 	Fishing and fisheries beyond territorial waters
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India** distributes legislative subjects between the Union and the States through three lists — **Union List (List I), State List (List II), and Concurrent List (List III).** The State List includes matters of local and administrative importance primarily under the jurisdiction of State Governments.
● **Prisons:** It is a **State subject** (Entry 4 of the State List). **So, point I is correct.**
● **Banking:** It falls under the **Union List** (Entry 45), as it involves the regulation of monetary and credit systems. **So,** **point II is not correct.**
● **Education:** Initially, it was in the State List, but later it was **shifted to the Concurrent List by the 42nd** **Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976** (Entry 25 of the Concurrent List). **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Agriculture:** It is specifically mentioned in **Entry 14 of the State List**. **So, point IV is correct.**
● **Betting and Gambling:** It is included under **Entry 34 of the State List.** **So, point V is correct.**
● **Fishing and fisheries beyond territorial waters:** It is included under **Entry 57 of the Union List. So, point VI** **is not correct.**
o The subject matter of 'Fisheries' falls under 'Entry 21' of the State List. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Important subjects under different Lists:**
● **Union List (List I):** Defence, Atomic Energy, Foreign Affairs, Railways, Banking, Insurance, Citizenship, Currency, Interstate Trade etc.
● **State List (List II):** Police, Public Order, Local Government, Public Health, Land, Agriculture, Fisheries, Betting and Gambling etc.
● **Concurrent List (List III):** Criminal Law, Marriage & Divorce, Education, Forests, Economic and Social Planning, Trade Unions, Bankruptcy etc.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
With reference to the President of India, consider
the following statements:
I. 	 The oath of office to the President is
administered by the Chief Justice of India.
II. 	 S/He can appoint any member of the Rajya
Sabha to preside over its proceedings when the office of the Chairman falls vacant.
III. 	S/He decides on questions regarding the
disqualification of Members of the Parliament, in consultation with the respective Presiding Officers of the Houses.
Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **President of India** is the head of the State and the supreme constitutional authority under **Article 52 of the** **Constitution.** His powers and functions extend across the executive, legislative, judicial, and emergency domains.
● The **oath or affirmation** by the President is prescribed under **Article 60** of the Constitution. It is administered by the **Chief Justice of India (CJI),** and in his absence, by the **senior-most judge** of the Supreme Court available. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The **Vice-President of India** is the **ex officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha** (Article 64).  When the office of the Chairman is vacant, the **Deputy Chairman** presides.  Only if **both offices are vacant,** the **President may appoint** **a member** of the Rajya Sabha to preside over its proceedings under Article 91(1). **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The decision regarding **the disqualification of Members of Parliament (MPs)** is governed by Article 103 (for MPs) and Article 192 (for MLAs).  In the case of Members of Parliament, if any question arises about disqualification **under Article 102(1) (other than defection)**, it is decided by the **President** after obtaining the **opinion of the Election Commission**, which is binding. The **Speaker** of the Lok Sabha is **not consulted** in such matters. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
questions.questionText:
With reference to the representation of Union
Territories in the Lok Sabha, consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Constitution of India empowers Parliament to determine the method of electing Lok Sabha representatives from Union Territories.
II. 	 Of the eight Union Territories, only Delhi,
Puducherry, and Jammu & Kashmir are represented in the Rajya Sabha.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Constitution of India has empowered the Parliament to prescribe the manner of choosing the** **representatives of the union territories in the Lok Sabha**. Accordingly, the Parliament has enacted the **Union** **Territories (Direct Election to the House of the People) Act, 1965**, by which the **members of Lok Sabha from** **the union territories are also chosen by direct election**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **All Union Territories (UTs) of India have representation in the Lok Sabha**; however, only a select few— namely, the **National Capital Territory of Delhi,** the Union Territory of **Puducherry,** and the Union Territory of **Jammu and Kashmir**—possess representation in the Rajya Sabha.  **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Union Territories representation in the Lok Sabha:**
● Delhi – 7 seats
● Puducherry – 1 seat
● Jammu & Kashmir – 5 seats
● Andaman and Nicobar Islands – 1 seat
● Chandigarh – 1 seat
● Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu – 2 seats
● Ladakh – 1 seat

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
Which of the following qualifications are
mentioned in the Constitution of India for a person to be appointed as the Governor of a State?
I. 	 S/He must have completed the age of thirty-
five years.
II. 	 A person domiciled in a State cannot be
appointed as the Governor of that State.
III. 	Only individuals who are citizens of India by
birth are eligible for appointment to the office of Governor.
IV. 	 To be eligible for appointment as a Governor,
s/he must be qualified to become a member of the State Legislature.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 153** of the Constitution says that there shall be a Governor for each State. The Governor is the **Nominal** **Executive Head of the State.** **Article 154** vests the **executive powers of the State** in the Governor, who exercises it either directly or through officers subordinate to him in accordance with the Constitution.
● The **Governor is appointed by the President** through a warrant under his hand and seal.
● **Article 157** of the Constitution mentions the **qualifications for appointment as Governor of a State.**
● There are only two qualifications mentioned in the Constitution for a Governor:
o s/he must be a citizen of India. o **s/he must have completed the age of thirty-five years**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● There is **no Constitutional requirement** that a person domiciled in a State cannot be appointed as the Governor of that State. **It is only a convention** that the **Governor should not belong to the State in which he is appointed**. Two conventions have been developed in this regard over the years. First, he should be an outsider; that is, he should not belong to the state where he is appointed. Second, while appointing the Governor, the President should consult the Chief Minister of the state concerned. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **In India, both a citizen by birth and a naturalised citizen are eligible to be appointed as a Governor. Thus, a** **naturalised citizen can hold the office of the Head of State in India.**
o This contrasts with the United States, where only a citizen by birth — and not a naturalised citizen — is eligible for certain high constitutional offices such as the President. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● To be eligible for being elected as the President, a person must qualify to become a member of the Lok Sabha. There is **no such requirement regarding the Governor**. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 If the Rajya Sabha resolves that it is necessary
in the national interest, Parliament becomes competent to legislate on subjects in the State list or Goods and Services Tax.
II. 	 The resolution must be passed by a two-thirds
majority of the members present and voting in the Rajya Sabha.
III. 	The resolution remains in force for six months
and can be renewed any number of times.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **India is a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic** having a **Parliamentary form of government**. The system is **federal in structure with unitary features**, ensuring a balance between central authority and state autonomy.
● The **Constitution of India** divides powers between the **Centre and the States**. This division covers **legislative**, **executive**, and **financial** powers to ensure a balanced distribution of authority.
● However, the **judicial power** is **not divided** between the two. Instead, the Constitution provides for an **integrated** **judicial system**, meaning that both **Central and State laws** are administered and enforced by the same set of courts.
● If the **Rajya Sabha declares** that it is necessary in the **national interest** for Parliament to make laws with respect to a matter in the **State List** or **Goods and Services Tax (Article 249)**, then Parliament becomes **competent to** **make laws** on that matter. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Such a **resolution must be supported** by a **two-thirds majority** of the members present and voting in the Rajya Sabha. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **resolution remains in force for one year** and can be **renewed any number of times**, but **not exceeding one** **year at a time**. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● The **laws made under this provision** cease to have effect **six months after the resolution has ceased to be in** **force**.
● This provision **does not restrict** the **power of a state legislature** to make laws on the same matter.
● However, in case of any **inconsistency between a state law and a parliamentary law**, the **parliamentary law** **shall prevail**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Indian Federal Structure:**
● Articles **245 to 255** in **Part XI** of the Constitution deal with the **legislative relations between** **the Centre and the States**. In addition to these, there are a few other articles that also relate to the same subject.
● Like other **federal constitutions**, the **Constitution of India** divides **legislative powers** between the **Centre and the States** with reference to both **territorial jurisdiction** and **subjects of** **legislation**.
● The Constitution also empowers **Parliament** to legislate on matters in the **State List** under **five** **exceptional circumstances**, ensuring flexibility within the federal framework. It further provides mechanisms for the **Centre’s control over State legislation** in specific cases.
● The **distribution of legislative powers** between the **Centre and the States** is designed to operate during **normal conditions**. However, in **abnormal or exceptional situations**, this scheme can be **modified or suspended**.
● In such circumstances, the **Constitution empowers Parliament** to make laws on matters that are otherwise within the **State List**. This ensures national unity, administrative flexibility, and effective governance during emergencies or special needs.
● Parliament can legislate on subjects in the **State List** under the following **five extraordinary** **circumstances**: o **When the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution** in the **national interest** under **Article 249**. o **When a national emergency** is in operation under **Article 250**. o **When two or more states** request Parliament to legislate on a **State subject** under **Article** **252**. o **When it is necessary to implement an international agreement, treaty, or convention** under **Article 253**. o **When the President’s Rule** is imposed in a state under **Article 356**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.questionText:
Consider the following legislation:
I.  	 The Indian Independence Act, 1947
II.  	 Government of India Act, 1935
III. 	Abolition of Privy Council Jurisdiction Act,
Which of the above Acts remained in force even after the commencement of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constitution of India,** as adopted on **November 26, 1949**, contained a Preamble, **395 Articles** and **8** **Schedules**. The Preamble was enacted after the entire Constitution was already enacted.
● Some provisions of the Constitution pertaining to citizenship, elections, provisional parliament, temporary and transitional provisions, and short title contained in Articles 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 60, 324, 366, 367, 379, 380, 388, 391, 392 and 393 came into force on November 26, 1949, itself.
● The remaining provisions (the major part) of the Constitution came into force on January 26, 1950. This day is referred to in the Constitution as the ‘date of its commencement’, and celebrated as the Republic Day.
● January 26 was chosen as the date of commencement of the Constitution to commemorate the 1930 Purna Swaraj declaration, adopted during the Lahore Session of the Indian National Congress in December 1929.
● **With the commencement of the Constitution, both the Indian Independence Act of 1947 and the Government** **of India Act of 1935**, along with all related amending or supplementary enactments, **were repealed. However, the** **Abolition of Privy Council Jurisdiction Act of 1949 remained in force. So, option III is correct and options I** **and II are not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Important Facts about the Constitution of India**
● The Constituent Assembly was formed in D**ecember 1946 under the Cabinet Mission Plan.**
● **First meeting: 9th December 1946 (Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha** as Temporary President).
● Symbol of Constituent Assembly – The **Elephant** was adopted as the official seal of the Constituent Assembly.
● Constitutional Advisor – **Sir B.N. Rau** was appointed as the Constitutional (Legal) Advisor to the Assembly.
● Secretary – **H.V.R. Iyengar** served as the Secretary of the Constituent Assembly.
● Chief Draftsman – **S.N. Mukherjee** was the Chief Draftsman of the Constitution.
● **Calligraphy** – P**rem Behari Narain Raizad**a handwrote the original Constitution in an elegant italic style.
● **Artistic Decoration** – The original document was illustrated and decorated by artists from Shantiniketan, led by Nandalal Bose and Beohar Rammanohar Sinha.
● **Illumination of Preamble** – The Preamble, calligraphed by Raizada, was ornamented and illuminated by Beohar Rammanohar Sinha.
● **Hindi Version** – The calligraphy of the Hindi version was done by Vasant Krishan Vaidya, with decoration and artistic work by Nandalal Bose.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following scenario:
The Panchayat Secretary of Rampur village has announced that only the members of the Gram Sabha can attend the upcoming meeting. Sita is 19 years old, lives in Rampur, and her name is included in the electoral roll of that village. Is Sita eligible to attend the Gram Sabha meeting?

options.a:
No, because the minimum age for Gram
Sabha membership is 21 years.
options.b:
Yes, because she is above 18 years of age,
resides in the village, and her name is in the electoral roll.
options.c:
No, because only elected members of the
Gram Panchayat can attend the Gram Sabha.
options.d:
Yes, because all women are members of the
Gram Sabha.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Gram Sabha** forms the core of India’s decentralized governance structure envisaged under the **73rd** **Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992**. It acts as the primary body of direct democracy at the village level, ensuring people’s participation in decision-making. Article 243 of the Constitution defines the Gram Sabha as the body consisting of **all persons registered in the electoral rolls** of a village within the area of the Gram Panchayat. It serves as a forum where villagers collectively discuss and decide on matters related to local governance, development, and accountability.
● The criteria for being a member of the Gram Sabha are:
1. The person must be **18 years of age or above**.
2. The person must be a **resident of the village.**
3. The person’s **name must be on the electoral roll** of that village.
● Since Sita fulfills all the conditions, she is a legitimate member of the Gram Sabha and has the right to **attend and** **participate** in its meetings. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Directive Principle of State Policy (DPSP)
Category Policy Implemented
I. - Securing a living wage
Socialist Code on Wages, 2019
II. - Promotion of cottage industry
Socialist One District One Product
III. - Promotion of educational and economic interests of SCs/STs
Gandhian Eklavya Model Residential Schools
IV.  Raise the
nutrition level
LiberalIntellectual
Poshan Abhiyan
In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSPs)**, enumerated in **Part IV (Articles 36–51)** of the Constitution of India, are **non-justiciable guidelines** that direct the State to establish a welfare state ensuring social, economic, and political justice. They are usually **classified** into three categories—**Socialistic, Gandhian, and Liberal-** **Intellectual**—based on their ideological origins and objectives.
● **Article 43 talks about securing a living wage,** a decent standard of life and social and cultural opportunities for all workers. It reflects **socialist ideals. Labour Code on Wages (2019), though, provides for wage reforms and** **social security for all workers, and does not legalise a living wage, but a minimum wage**.  **So, row I is not** **correctly matched.**
● Promotion of **cottage and small-scale industries (Article 43)** is a **Gandhian** principle, reflecting Gandhi’s vision of self-sufficient villages. The **One District One Product (ODOP)** scheme promotes local crafts and rural entrepreneurship, **aligning with Gandhian ideals. So, row II is not correctly matched.**
● **Article 46** directs the State to promote **educational and economic interests** of the weaker sections, particularly **SCs and STs.** This belongs to the **Gandhian** category. The **Eklavya Model Residential Schools** scheme directly implements this directive. **So, row III is correctly matched.**
● **Article 47** directs the State to **raise nutrition levels** and improve public health, **a socialist principle** aimed at achieving a **welfare state.** The **Poshan Abhiyan (2018)** is a flagship initiative fulfilling this goal. **So, row IV is** **not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements is **not**
correct with regard to the Supreme Court of India?

options.a: It acts as the guardian of the Constitution.
options.b:
The President determines the actual strength
of the Court from time to time.
options.c:
It acts both as a Federal Court and as a final
court of appeal.
options.d:
It possesses original jurisdiction in disputes
involving Fundamental Rights.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Articles 124 to 147 in Part V of the Constitution of India deal with the organisation, independence, jurisdiction, powers, procedures and so on of the Supreme Court.
● Originally, the strength of the Supreme Court was fixed at eight Judges, comprising the Chief Justice of India and seven other Judges**. The Constitution vests in Parliament the authority to increase the number of Judges of** **the Supreme Court.** In exercise of this power, Parliament has progressively raised the sanctioned strength: to ten in 1956, thirteen in 1960, seventeen in 1977, twenty-five in 1986, thirty in 2008, and thirty-three in 2019. At present, the Supreme Court consists of thirty-four Judges, including the Chief Justice of India. **So, option (b) is not correct.**
● The **Constitution** has constituted the **Supreme Court** as the **guarantor and defender of the Fundamental Rights** of citizens. The **Supreme Court** is empowered to **issue writs,** including **habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition,** **quo warranto, and certiorari**, for the **enforcement of Fundamental Rights** of an aggrieved citizen.
● The Supreme Court is the **final interpreter and guardian of the Constitution** and the **guarantor of the** **Fundamental Rights** of citizens. **So, option (a) is correct.**
● The Supreme Court has **original jurisdiction**, meaning an aggrieved citizen can **directly approach the Court**, not necessarily by way of appeal. However, this **writ jurisdiction** is **not exclusive**, as **High Courts** are also empowered to issue writs for enforcing **Fundamental Rights**. Thus, when Fundamental Rights are violated, the aggrieved party may move **either the High Court or the Supreme Court directly**. **So, option (d) is correct.**
● The **Constitution** has conferred **extensive jurisdiction** and **vast powers** on the **Supreme Court**. It functions not only as a **Federal Court**, like the **American Supreme Court**, but also as a **final court of appeal**, similar to the **British House of Lords** (the Upper House of the British Parliament).  **So, option (c) is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I** Acts of Parliament do not automatically apply to all states and regions of India in the same manner. **Statement-II** In the case of Assam, the application of Parliamentary laws is subject to the President’s decision.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct
and Statement–II explains Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are
correct, but Statement–II does **not** explain Statement–I
options.c:
Statement–I is correct, but Statement–II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **doctrine of territorial nexus** is connected to **Article 245** of the Constitution, which defines the **extent of laws** **made by Parliament and State Legislatures**.
● Under this Article, the **Parliament** is empowered to **make laws for the whole or any part of the territory of** **India**. Moreover, it can also make **extra-territorial legislation**, meaning that **Parliamentary laws** can apply **not** **only within India** but also to **Indian citizens and their property located outside India**.
● The **President** has the power to **make regulations** for the **peace, progress, and good government** of certain **Union** **Territories** (**Ladakh)**. A **regulation made by the President** has the **same force and effect as an Act of** **Parliament**. It can also **repeal or amend** any **Parliamentary Act** applicable to these Union Territories. **So,** **statement II is not correct.**
● The **Constitution**, under the **Sixth Schedule**, contains **special provisions** for the **administration of tribal areas** in the four **North-Eastern states** — **Assam**, **Meghalaya**, **Tripura**, and **Mizoram**.
● The **Acts of Parliament or the State Legislature** do not apply to autonomous districts and autonomous regions or **apply with specified modifications and exceptions.**  **So, statement I is correct.** o The power of direction, in this regard, lies either with the President or the Governor. Thus, **in the case of Assam,** **it lies with the Governor, both in respect of acts of Parliament or the state legislature**. **In the case of** **Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram, it lies with the President in respect of acts of Parliament and the Governor** **in respect of acts of the state legislature. So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
questions.questionText:
If the President issues a proclamation imposing
a Financial Emergency in India, then which of the following statements best reflects the consequence(s) of it?
I. 	 The transfer of finances/funds from the Centre
to the States gets automatically cancelled.
II. 	 Prior approval of the President would be
required for the introduction of money bills in all State Legislatures.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Article 360 of the Constitution of India empowers the President to proclaim a Financial Emergency **if the financial** **stability or credit of India or any part of its territory is threatened.**
o A **proclamation** declaring a financial emergency **must be approved by both Houses of Parliament** **within two months from the date of its issue**. A resolution approving the proclamation of financial emergency can be passed by either House of Parliament only **by a simple majority.** o **Once approved** by both Houses of Parliament, the **Financial Emergency continues indefinitely till it is** **revoked.**
● The following are the consequences of the proclamation of a Financial Emergency by the President:
o The Centre can give directions to any State to observe such canons of financial propriety as may be specified in the directions. o Any such direction may include the following:
▪ the reduction of salaries and allowances of all or any class of persons serving in the state; and ▪ the **reservation of all money bills or other financial bills for the consideration of the President** **after they are passed by the legislature of the State (not before the introduction). So, statement** **II is not correct.** o The President may issue directions for the reduction of salaries and allowances of (a) all or any class of persons serving the Union; and (b) the judges of the Supreme Court and the High Court.
● **There is no automatic cancellation of provisions regarding the transfer of finances/funds from the Centre to** **the States.** **So, statement I is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Indian polity, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 The Constitution of India lays down the
tenure of both Houses of Parliament.
II.	 The Constitution of India prescribes the
quorum to be one-tenth of the total number of members of the House.
III. 	The premature dissolution of the Lok Sabha
is generally not subject to judicial review.
Which of the statements given above is/are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 83(2) of the Constitution sets the Lok Sabha’s term at five years from its first meeting, unless it is** **dissolved earlier**. In contrast, the Rajya Sabha is described as a permanent body that cannot be dissolved, according to Article 83(1). While the **Constitution** does **not specify** the exact term of its members, it provides that **one-third** **of them retire every two years.** **Parliament later defined** this through the **Representation of the People Act,** **1951**, **fixing the term of Rajya Sabha members at six years**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **dissolution of the Lok Sabha** is a political and constitutional act carried out by the President under **Article** **85(2)(b)** of the Constitution, based on the advice of the Council of Ministers. It marks the end of the Lok Sabha's term before its scheduled five years and leads to general elections. **Courts usually do not interfere in dissolution** **matters due to the doctrine of separation of powers**. **So, statement III is correct.**
● **Article 100(3)** states that, “**Until Parliament by law otherwise provides**, the **quorum to constitute a meeting of** **either House of Parliament shall be one-tenth of the total number of members of the House**.” This means the minimum number of members required to conduct business is **10%** of the total strength of the House. **So, statement** **II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
Consider the following Public Interest Litigations
(PILs):
I. - Petition for early hearing of cases pending in subordinate courts
II. 	 Petition against the police for refusal to register
a case
III. 	 Petition from a jailed convict complaining of
harassment
IV. 	 Petition alleging violation of labour laws in an
individual case In how many of the above instances is a PIL liable to be dismissed?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The concept of **Public Interest Litigation (PIL)** originated and developed in the **USA during the 1960s**. In the United States, it was designed to **provide legal representation to previously unrepresented groups and interests**.
● In **India**, the concept of **PIL** is **closely linked to judicial activism**. Its emergence is mainly attributed to the **judicially active role of the Supreme Court**, making **PIL an outcome and expression of judicial activism**.
● The following **categories of cases will not be entertained as PILs**:
o Landlord–tenant disputes o Service matters, and those related to pension or gratuity o Complaints against Central/State Government departments or local bodies, with some exceptions. o Admissions to medical or educational institutions o **Petitions for early hearing of pending cases** in **High Courts or subordinate courts. So, point I is** **correct.**
● The **Supreme Court** framed a set of **guidelines** for entertaining **letters or petitions** received as **Public Interest** **Litigations (PILs)**. These guidelines were **modified in 1993 and 2003**. According to them, **only the following** **categories** of letters or petitions will ordinarily be **entertained as PILs**:
o Bonded labour matters o Neglected children o **Non-payment of minimum wages**, **exploitation of casual workers**, and **violation of labour laws** (except in individual cases)**. So, point IV is correct.** o **Petitions from jails complaining of harassment**, seeking premature release, release after 14 years, death in jail, transfer, release on personal bond, or speedy trial as a fundamental right. **So, point III is not correct.** o **Petitions against police** for **refusal to register a case**, harassment, or death in police custody. **So, point** **II is not correct.** o Petitions against atrocities on women, especially bride harassment, dowry deaths, rape, murder, or kidnapping. o Petitions alleging harassment or torture of villagers by co-villagers or police, particularly from the Scheduled Castes, the Scheduled Tribes, and the economically backward classes
o Petitions on environmental pollution, ecological imbalance, food adulteration, drug safety, preservation of heritage and culture, antiquities, forests, and wildlife, or other matters of public importance o Petitions from riot victims o Family pension matters
● **Article 226** grants High Courts the power to issue writs to enforce constitutional rights (questioning the constitutionality of a law). PILs can also be filed in high courts under this provision, and they have been widely used to address matters that affect public interest.
o Ordinarily, the High Court should not entertain a writ petition by way of PIL questioning the constitutionality or validity of a statute or a statutory rule. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About the Public Interest Litigations (PILs):**
● The **PIL was introduced in India in the early 1980s**, with **Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer** and **Justice P.N. Bhagwati** being the **pioneer** of this transformative concept.
● The **PIL** is also known by other terms such as **Social Action Litigation (SAL)**, **Social Interest** **Litigation (SIL)**, and **Class Action Litigation (CAL)**.
● The **Public Interest Litigation (PIL)** has emerged as a transformative instrument of justice delivery in India.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Urban Local Body
Purpose
I. - Notified Area Committee
Administration of small towns with limited civic functions
II. - Town Area Committee
Administration of areas around large public enterprises for providing civic amenities.
III. - Cantonment Board
Administration of the civilian population in cantonment areas
IV. - Township Administration of areas that are fast developing but not yet fulfilling all conditions for a municipality
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) are institutions that look after the administration of urban areas in India. Besides municipal corporations and municipalities, there are **special-purpose urban bodies** like **Notified Area** **Committees, Town Area Committees, Cantonment Boards, and Townships**. These bodies are created for specific types of urban regions that do not fit into the usual municipal pattern.
● A **Notified Area Committee** is formed for two kinds of areas —
1. **Fast-developing towns** due to industrialisation, and
2. Towns that are **important but do not yet meet all legal conditions** for becoming a municipality.
● It is set up by a **government notification** (not by law), and only those parts of the municipal law that are mentioned in the notification apply. It is **not an elected body** — all its members, including the chairman, are **nominated by** **the state government**.  **So, pair I is not correctly matched**
● A **Town Area Committee** is actually formed for **small towns**. It is a **semi-municipal authority** with **limited civic** **functions**, such as street lighting, drainage, roads, and sanitation. It is created by a **separate state law**, and members can be elected, nominated, or both. **So, pair II is not correctly matched**
● A **Cantonment Board** is created by the **Central Government** under the **Cantonments Act, 2006,** for the municipal administration of **civilian populations living in military cantonment areas**. It is administered by the **Defence** **Ministry**, unlike other ULBs, which are under state governments. It consists of **both elected and nominated** **members**. The military officer commanding the station is the president, and elected members serve for five years. **So, pair III is correctly matched**
● A **Township** is created by **large public sector enterprises** to provide civic amenities like housing, roads, water supply, and sanitation to their employees living near industrial plants. It has **no elected members**. Administration is handled by a **town administrator** appointed by the enterprise, supported by engineers and staff. It is essentially an **extension of the enterprise’s bureaucratic structure**. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched** **So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the
National Legal Services Authority (NALSA):
I. - It is a constitutional body.
II. 	 The Chief Justice of India is the Executive
Chairman of NALSA.
III.	 There is no annual income limit for availing
free legal aid under the Act.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **National Legal Services Authority (NALSA)** was constituted under the **Legal Services Authorities Act,** **1987,** to monitor and evaluate the implementation of legal aid programmes and to lay down policies and principles for providing legal services under the Act. It is a **statutory body,** not a constitutional one. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● It coordinates and monitors the functioning of various Legal Services Institutions established across India to ensure the effective implementation of legal aid programmes.
● The **National Legal Services Authority (NALSA)** was formed on 9th November 1995. The **Chief Justice of India** is the **Patron-in-Chief,** NALSA. **A Judge of the Supreme Court** of India is nominated as the **Executive** **Chairman** of **NALSA**.  **So, statement II is not correct.**
● In every state, a **State Legal Services Authority** and in every High Court, a **High Court Legal Services** **Committee** have been established.
● Similarly, **District Legal Services Authorities** and **Taluk Legal Services Committees** have been constituted in various districts and taluks to implement the policies and directions of NALSA.
● These bodies work to **provide free legal services to the people** and to **organise Lok Adalats** for the speedy and amicable settlement of disputes, thereby ensuring access to justice for all, particularly for the weaker and marginalized sections of society.
● **Persons eligible for getting free legal services include:**
o Women and children o Members of the Scheduled Castes (SC) and the Scheduled Tribes (ST) o Industrial workmen o Victims of mass disaster, violence, flood, drought, earthquake, or industrial disaster o Disabled persons o Persons in custody (including those in protective homes, juvenile homes, or psychiatric hospitals) o Persons whose annual income does not exceed ₹1,00,000 (in the Supreme Court Legal Services Committee, the income limit is ₹5,00,000) o Victims of trafficking in human beings or begar (forced labor). **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Key Functions of  National Legal Services Authority (NALSA):** As per **Section 4** of the **Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987**, **NALSA** has been tasked with **formulating policies and schemes** to provide effective and economical legal aid, **allocating** **funds to State and District Authorities,** and promoting social justice through litigation in areas such as consumer protection and environmental law.
● They **organize legal aid camps**, especially for marginalized communities, encourage alternative dispute resolution, conduct research on legal services for the poor, and ensure adherence to Fundamental Duties.
● Additionally, they **monitor and evaluate legal aid programmes, provide grants to voluntary** **organizations**, **promote clinical legal education, spread legal literacy,** support disadvantaged groups, and coordinate with various legal services committees to ensure proper implementation of legal aid initiatives.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements is correct
with regard to a Bill proposing the diminution of the area of a Union Territory (UT) of India?

options.a: It can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha.
options.b:
Prior recommendation of the President
is necessary before presenting the Bill in Parliament.
options.c:
Such a Bill must be referred to the concerned
UT's Legislature, though the Parliament is not bound by the views of the Legislature.
options.d:
Such a Bill must be passed by a special
majority in the Parliament.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Under **Article 3** of the **Constitution of India**, Parliament has the authority to reorganise the territory of India. However, the Constitution also prescribes certain procedural requirements before such a Bill can be introduced in Parliament.
● **Article 3 of the Constitution of India** authorises the Parliament to:
o form a new state by the separation of territory from any State or by uniting two or more States or parts of States or by uniting any territory to a part of any State; o increase the area of any State; o diminish the area of any State; o alter the boundaries of any State; o alter the name of any State.
● However, Article 3 lays down two conditions in this regard:
o A bill contemplating the above changes can be introduced in the Parliament **only with the prior** **recommendation of the President**; Thus, it **can be introduced in either the Lok Sabha or the Rajya** **Sabha. So, option (a) is not correct and option (b) is correct.** o Before recommending the bill, the President has to refer the same to the State Legislature concerned for expressing its views within a specified period.
● The **President (or Parliament) is not bound by the views of the State Legislatur**e and **may either accept or** **reject them**, even if the views are received in time. Further, it is not necessary to make a fresh reference to the State Legislature every time an amendment to the bill is moved and accepted in Parliament.
● **In case of a Union Territory, no reference need be made to the concerned Legislature to ascertain its views** **and the Parliament can itself take any action as it deems fit. So, option (c) is not correct.**
● **Article 4** declares that laws made for admission or establishment of new States (under Article 2) and formation of new States and alteration of areas, boundaries or names of existing States (under Articles 3) are **not to be considered** **as amendments of the Constitution under Article 368**. This means that such laws can be passed by a **simple** **majority and by the ordinary legislative process**. **So, option (d) is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I** Legislation placed under the Ninth Schedule of the Constitution of India is not entirely shielded from judicial scrutiny. **Statement-II** As per the I. R. Coelho judgment, laws inserted in the Ninth Schedule on or after 24 April 1973 are subject to judicial review.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct
and Statement–II explains Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are
correct, but Statement–II does **not** explain Statement–I
