options.c:
Statement–I is correct, but Statement–II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **First Constitutional Amendment Act, 1951,** inserted **Article 31B** and the **Ninth Schedule** in the Constitution of India. It provides that the **acts and regulations included in the Ninth Schedule cannot be challenged or** **invalidated** on the ground that they **contravene Fundamental Rights**.
● Originally, in **1951**, the Ninth Schedule contained **13 Acts and regulations**, mainly relating to **land reforms and** **the abolition of the zamindari system**. At present, it includes around **282 laws;** most of the **state laws** deal with **land reforms**, while those of **Parliament** cover **other matters**.
● In the **Kesavananda Bharati Case (1973),** the **Supreme Court** held that even though Article 31B protects laws placed in the **Ninth Schedule** from being challenged for violating **Fundamental Rights**, such laws are **open to** **challenge** if they **violate the Basic Structure** of the Constitution.
● In the **Waman Rao Case (1980),** the Court clarified that **laws inserted into the Ninth Schedule after April 24,** **1973** (the date of the **Kesavananda Bharati** judgment) would be **valid only if they do not damage the Basic** **Structure** of the Constitution.
● In the **I.R. Coelho Case (2007)** the Supreme Court **reaffirmed** that there can be **no blanket immunity** from **judicial review** for laws placed under the Ninth Schedule. **So, statement I is correct.**
● It declared **judicial review** a **Basic Feature** of the Constitution. Therefore, even laws placed in the Ninth Schedule **after April 24, 1973** can be **challenged in court** if they **violate Fundamental Rights** (especially **Articles 14, 19,** **and 21**) or the **Basic Structure**. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct and Statement–II explains Statement–I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Conclusions of the I.R. Coelho Judgment:**
● A law that **abrogates or abridges rights guaranteed by Part III** may or may not violate the Basic Structure. If it does, whether through an amendment or insertion in the Ninth Schedule, it can be **invalidated** **through judicial review**. The **“direct impact and effect” (rights test)** determines validity, not the form of amendment but its consequences.
● The **validity of each constitutional amendment** must be judged on its own merits. The **impact on** **Fundamental Rights** determines whether it destroys the Basic Structure.
● All **amendments made on or after April 24, 1973**, that place laws in the Ninth Schedule must be **tested** against the **Basic Features** of the Constitution, especially **Articles 14, 19, and 21**. Even if included in the Ninth Schedule, a law can be **struck down** if it **damages or destroys the Basic Structure**.
● Protection for laws in the Ninth Schedule is **not blanket protection**. Courts must **examine the nature and** **extent of Fundamental Right infringement** using the **“rights test”** and **“essence of the right test.”** If the infringement affects the **Basic Structure**, the law **cannot claim immunity**.
● If the Supreme Court has already **upheld the validity of a Ninth Schedule law**, it **cannot be reopened** for challenge. However, if a law **previously struck down** is **later inserted into the Ninth Schedule (after** **1973)**, it **can be challenged** again for **violating the Basic Structure**.
● Any **actions taken or transactions finalised** under the impugned laws will **remain valid** and **cannot be** **reopened** for challenge.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements best
represents the concept of "secularism" as enshrined in the Constitution of India?

options.a:
The State accords equal respect and
recognition to all religions.
options.b:
The State maintains a complete separation
between religion and personal laws.
options.c:
The State gives preferential treatment to
minority religions to ensure social balance.
options.d:
The State excludes all forms of religion from
the public sphere.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In the **Western model of secularism**, particularly in countries like France and the United States, secularism implies a strict **separation of religion and the State**, often leading to the exclusion of religion from public life. In contrast, the **Indian model of secularism** does not advocate complete separation but emphasizes **equal respect for all** **religions**.
● The Constitution of India ensures that the State is **not aligned with any particular religion, nor is it irreligious.** Instead, it ensures that citizens of all faiths enjoy equal freedom of conscience and the right to practice, profess, and propagate religion under Articles 25–28. This reflects the unique Indian approach where the State may intervene in religious matters, not to promote or suppress them, but to ensure reform, equality, and public welfare. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Constitutional Provisions Reflecting the Secular Character of India:**
● **Preamble** – The word “Secular” was inserted by the **42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976**. It also guarantees liberty of belief, faith, and worship to all citizens.
● **Equality and Non-Discrimination:**
o **Article 14** – Equality before law and equal protection of the laws
o **Article 15** – Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth o **Article 16** – Equality of opportunity in matters of public employment
● **Freedom of Religion**
o **Article 25** – Freedom of conscience and right to profess, practice, and propagate religion o **Article 26** – Rights of religious denominations to manage their own religious affairs o **Article 27** – Freedom from payment of taxes for promotion of a particular religion o **Article 28** – No religious instruction in State-maintained educational institutions
● **Cultural and Educational Rights**
o **Article 29** – Right to conserve distinct language, script, or culture o **Article 30** – Right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice
● **Directive Principle**
o **Article 44** – Directive to secure a Uniform Civil Code for all citizens

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC):
I. 	 The appointments are made on the recommendation of a six-member committee headed by the Central Home Minister.
II. 	 After their tenure, the chairperson and
members are ineligible for further employment under the Central or State Government.
III. 	It can suo moto investigate negligence by
public servants in preventing human rights violations.
IV. 	 It cannot recommend compensation or
prosecution against guilty public servants.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)** is a **statutory** (and not a constitutional) body. It was established in 1993 under a legislation enacted by the Parliament, namely, the **Protection of Human Rights Act,** **1993.**
● The commission is a **multi-member body consisting of a chairperson and five members.**
● The chairperson and members are **appointed by the president on the recommendations of a six-member** **committee consisting of the prime minister as its head,** the Speaker of the Lok Sabha, the Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, Leaders of the Opposition in both the Houses of Parliament and the Central Home Minister. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● Further, a sitting judge of the Supreme Court or a sitting chief justice of a high court can be appointed only after consultation with the chief justice of India.
● The **Chairperson should be a retired Chief Justice of India** **or a judge of the Supreme Court** and **members** **should be a serving or retired judge of the Supreme Court,** a **serving or retired Chief Justice of a High Court** **and three persons (out of which at least one should be a woman) having knowledge or practical experience** **with respect to human rights.**
● The chairperson and members **hold office for a term of three years or until they attain the age of 70 years,** **whichever is earlier.**
● They are eligible for re-appointment. **After their tenure, the chairperson and members are not eligible for** **further employment under the Central or a state government. So, statement II is correct.**
● **The President has the authority to remove the chairperson or a member** under certain circumstances.
● The **salaries, allowances and other conditions of service of the chairperson or a member are determined by** **the Central government.** But, they cannot be varied to his disadvantage after his appointment.
● It has the **power to inquire** into any **violation of human rights or negligence** in preventing such violations **by** **public servants**, either on its initiative **(suo motu),** based on petitions presented to it, or by order of a court.  **So,** **statement III is correct.**
● **NHRC can recommend:**
o payment of compensation or damages to victims, and o initiation of prosecution or disciplinary action against erring public servants. **So, statement IV is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) recently concluded its 3rd Online Short Term Internship Programme (OSTI) of 2025-2026.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Constituent Assembly:
I. 	 In each British Indian province, the seats in the
Constituent Assembly were allocated among Muslims, Sikhs, and the General category in proportion to their respective populations.
II. 	 Representatives of each community were to
be elected by members of that community in the provincial legislative assemblies, using the first-past-the-post system.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Under the **Cabinet Mission Plan (Constituent Assembly scheme, Nov 1946),** the Assembly was explicitly **partly** **elected and partly nominated** — members for British Indian provinces were to be **elected (indirectly)** by provincial legislative assemblies, while representatives of the **princely state**s were to be **nominated** by their rulers.
● Of the **296 seats allotted to British India**, **292** were to come from the **eleven governors’ provinces** and **only 4** from the four **Chief Commissioners’ provinces** (one each).
● The scheme provided that the **representatives of the princely states were to be nominated by the heads of the** **princely states,** which is why the Assembly had a nominated component.
● **Composition of the Constituent Assembly** o The Constituent Assembly was to have **389 members** — **296 from British India** and **93 from princely states**. o **Seats were allotted in proportion to population**, roughly **one seat per million people**. o Seats in each British province were divided among **Muslims, Sikhs, and General (others)** in proportion to their population. **So, statement I is correct.** o **Community representatives** were to be **elected by their community members** in the **provincial legislative** **assemblies**, through **proportional representation by single transferable vote**. **So, statement II is not correct.** o **Princely state representatives** were to be **nominated by the rulers** of those states. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Central Council of Local Government:
I. 	 It is an advisory body, constituted under
Article 263 of the Constitution of India.
II. 	 It deals with the matters of urban local
government only.
III. 	The Union Minister of Housing and Urban
Affairs serves as the Chairperson of the Council.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Central Council of Local Government** was established in **1954. It is a constitutional body constituted** **under Article 263 of the Constitution through a Presidential order. So, statement I is correct**
● Initially named the Central Council of Local Self-Government, the term “self-government” was later considered redundant and was replaced with “government” during the 1980s. Until 1958, the Council addressed issues related to **both urban and rural local governance**, but since then, its focus has been limited to matters concerning **urban** **local government. So, statement II is correct.**
● The Council functions as an advisory body comprising the **Union Minister for Urban Development**, who serves as its **Chairman** and the Ministers in charge of local self-government from various States. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
With reference to a Finance Bill in the Union
Budget, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha.
II. 	 The Rajya Sabha cannot reject or amend the
bill.
III. 	There is a provision for a joint sitting in case
of disagreement over the bill.
IV. 	 Unlike the Appropriation Bill, the amendments
seeking to reject or reduce a tax can be moved in the case of a finance bill.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Finance Bill** is introduced **to give effect to the financial proposals of the Government of India** for the following year. It is **subject to all the conditions applicable to a Money Bill, i.e.:**
o It **can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha, and that too on the recommendation of the President**. **So,** **statement I is correct.** o Every such bill is considered to be a government bill and **can be introduced only by a minister.** o After the bill is passed by the Lok Sabha, it is transmitted to the Rajya Sabha for its consideration. o The **Rajya Sabha has restricted powers** with regard to a finance bill. It cannot reject or amend a money bill. It **can only make recommendations.** It **must return the bill to the Lok Sabha within 14 days,** whether with or without recommendations. **So, statement II is correct.** o The **Lok Sabha can either accept or reject all or any of the recommendations** of the Rajya Sabha. o If the Lok Sabha accepts any recommendation, the bill is then deemed to have been passed by both Houses in the modified form. If the Lok Sabha does not accept any recommendation, the bill is then deemed to have passed by both the Houses in the form originally passed by the Lok Sabha without any change.
o If the Rajya Sabha does not return the bill to the Lok Sabha within 14 days, the bill is deemed to have been passed by both the Houses in the form originally passed by the Lok Sabha. Thus, the **Lok Sabha has more** **powers than the Rajya Sabha with regard to a finance bill.** o The Lok Sabha holds overriding powers with regard to a Finance Bill, and **no joint sitting is allowed** to resolve disagreements since the Rajya Sabha can only make recommendations, which the Lok Sabha may accept or reject. **So, statement III is not correct.** o Finally, **when a finance bill is presented to the President**, he may either give his assent to the bill or withhold his assent to the bill, but **cannot return the bill for reconsideration of the Houses.** o Normally, the President gives his assent to a Finance Bill as it is introduced in Parliament with his prior permission.
● Unlike the Appropriation Bill, the amendments (seeking to reject or reduce a tax) can be moved in the case of a finance bill. According to the Provisional Collection of Taxes Act of 1931, the Finance Bill must be enacted (i.e., passed by the Parliament and assented to by the president) within 75 days. **The Finance Act legalises the income** **side of the budget and completes the process of the enactment of the budget. So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Financial bills** are those bills that **deal with fiscal matters**, that is, revenue or expenditure. However, the Constitution uses the term ‘financial bill’ in a technical sense. Financial bills are of three kinds:
● **Money bills–Article 110**
● **Financial bills (I)–Article 117 (1)**
● **Financial bills (II)–Article 117 (3)**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
With respect to the reservation in promotions
under Article 16(4A) of the Constitution of India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Reservation for OBCs is confined to initial
appointments only and there is no provision for reservation in promotions.
II. 	 The 85th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2001, inserted the provision of “consequential seniority” to allow reserved category candidates to retain seniority over general category peers.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 16** of the Constitution of India guarantees **equality of opportunity in matters of public employment.** Over time, several constitutional amendments and Supreme Court judgments have clarified the **scope of** **reservation in promotions** and the conditions governing it.
● **Applicability of reservation in promotions (Article 16(4A)):** The **77th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1995,** inserted Article 16(4A), empowering the State to make provisions for reservation in promotions in favour of **Scheduled Castes (SCs)** and **Scheduled Tribes (STs)** that are not adequately represented in the services of the State. This provision **does not apply to OBCs.** Reservation for OBCs is confined to initial appointments under Article 16(4).  **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Consequential seniority and conditions:** The **85th Amendment Act, 2001,** inserted the provision of **“consequential seniority”**. Consequential seniority allows reserved category candidates to retain seniority over general category peers. If a reserved category candidate is promoted before a general category candidate because of a reservation in promotion, then for subsequent promotion, the reserved candidate retains seniority. However, as laid down by the Supreme Court in **M. Nagaraj v. Union of India (2006)**, the State must collect quantifiable data showing:
o the **backwardness** of the class, o their **inadequate representation**, and o the overall **administrative efficiency** not being affected. These are the preconditions before granting a reservation in promotions with consequential seniority. **So, statement II is correct** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Indian Parliament, consider
the following statements:
I. 	 Both Houses have equal powers to create
new All-India Services common to both the Centre and States.
II. 	 Both Houses participate equally in the
impeachment process of the President.
III. 	Both Houses have equal power in removing
the Council of Ministers by passing a noconfidence motion.
IV. 	 Both Houses have equal authority in
approving constitutional amendments.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Rajya Sabha has been given four exclusive or special powers that are not enjoyed by the Lok Sabha:**
o **It can authorise the Parliament to make a law on a subject enumerated in the State List (Article 249).** o It can authorise the Parliament to create new All-India Services common to both the Centre and states (Article 312). **So, statement I is not correct.** o It alone can initiate a move for the removal of the Vice President. In other words, a resolution for the removal of the Vice-President can be introduced only in the Rajya Sabha and not in the Lok Sabha (Article 67). o If a proclamation is issued by the President for imposing national emergency or president’s rule or financial emergency at a time when the Lok Sabha has been dissolved or the dissolution of the Lok Sabha takes place within the period allowed for its approval, then the proclamation can remain effective even if it is approved by the Rajya Sabha alone (Articles 352, 356 and 360).
● **The President of India can be impeached under Article 61 for violating the Constitution.** The process requires a resolution passed by each House of Parliament with a two-thirds majority of members present and voting, following at least fourteen days' prior notice and an investigation. **Both the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha play** **equal roles in this constitutional procedure.** The President has the right to defend themselves during the investigation, and removal takes effect once both Houses pass the resolution. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The Rajya Sabha cannot remove the council of ministers by passing a no-confidence motion.** This is because the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible only to the Lok Sabha. But, the Rajya Sabha can discuss and criticise the policies and activities of the government. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● **Article 368 governs the process of constitutional amendments.** Both Houses of Parliament must pass the amendment bill with a special majority, meaning a majority of the total membership and at least two-thirds of members present and voting. No joint sitting is allowed in case of disagreement, and **both Houses have equal** **authority in constitutional amendments.** **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
The 44th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1978,
removed the Right to Property as a Fundamental Right. Which of the following were the consequences of this development?
I. 	 Compensation is not guaranteed for the
acquisition of land under personal cultivation even if it lies within statutory ceiling limits.
II. 	 The right does not protect against deprivation
of property by either legislative or executive action.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Originally, the Right to Property** was a **Fundamental Right** guaranteed under Articles 19(1)(f) and 31 of the Constitution of India. However, due to **repeated conflicts** between the legislature and the judiciary over compensation and land reform laws, the **44th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1978,** abolished it as a Fundamental Right. The same amendment inserted **Article 300A in Part XII,** making the Right to Property a **constitutional** **(legal) right** instead.
● **Compensation under Article 31A:** Although the Right to Property is no longer a Fundamental Right, two provisions still **guarantee compensation** under specific circumstances:
o When the State acquires property of a **minority educational institution (Article 30).** o When the State acquires land under **personal cultivation** **within statutory ceiling limits (Article 31A).** **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Protection under Article 300A:** The Right to Property under Article 300A ensures that no person shall be deprived of property **except by authority of law.** This means it protects against **executive action not backed** by law. However, it does not protect against legislative action, since **Parliament or State Legislatures** can curtail or modify the right through an ordinary law. Therefore, the right protects against executive action only, not both executive and legislative actions. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
If India chooses to engage in war with another
nation, which authority possesses the ultimate power to declare the war?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Under the Indian Constitution, **subject to parliamentary approval, the power to declare war or conclude peace** **rests with the President of India**, as per **Article 53**, which vests the **executive power** of the Union **in the** **President**. However, this power is exercised in **accordance with the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers** headed by the Prime Minister (Article 74).
o The President is the supreme commander of the defence forces of India. In that capacity, he appoints the chiefs of the Army, the Navy, and the Air Force.
● The **National Security Council** (NSC) of India is a pivotal body, entrusted with advising the Prime Minister's Office (PMO) on multifaceted issues concerning the nation's security landscape.
o It was established in 1998, following India's nuclear tests, to provide a more structured and comprehensive approach to national security.
● The **Chief of Defence Staff** of the Indian Armed Forces (CDS) is the **military head** and **permanent Chairman** of the **Chiefs of Staff Committee** (COSC) of the Indian Armed Forces.
o The Chief of Defence Staff is the highest-ranking uniformed officer on active duty in the Indian military and chief military adviser to the Minister of Defence. o The Chief also heads the Department of Military Affairs. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Attorney General of India,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Attorney General enjoys all the privileges
and immunities available to Members of Parliament.
II. 	 No court can decline to hear the Attorney
General when he or she appears to make an official submission.
III. 	A private individual can initiate contempt
of court proceedings only with the written consent of the Attorney General.
IV. 	 There is no prescribed age of retirement for the
Attorney General of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Attorney General of India** is the highest law officer of the country, established under **Article 76** of the Constitution. He acts as the chief legal advisor to the Government of India and performs **legal duties assigned by** **the President.**
● As per **Article 76(1)**, the Attorney General is appointed by the **President of India.**  The **qualifications prescribed** **are that the person must be qualified to be appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court**, i.e.:
o Be a citizen of India, and o Have been a **judge** of some **High Court for five years**, or an **advocate** of a High Court for t**en years,** or, in the **opinion of the President, be an eminent jurist.**
● In the performance of his official duties, **he has the right** to **speak** and t**o take part** in the **proceedings of both** the **Houses of Parliament** or their **joint sitting** and **any committee** of the Parliament of which he may be named a member, but without a right to vote. He **enjoys all the privileges** and **immunities** that are **available to a member** **of Parliament. So, statement I is correct.**
● **Article 76(3)** explicitly states that in the performance of his/her duties, the Attorney General shall have the **right** **of audience in all courts in the territory of India**. This means all courts are constitutionally bound to hear the AG when acting in an official capacity. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Section 15** of the **Contempt of Courts Act, 1971** provides the procedure for initiating **criminal contempt** (other than contempt committed in the court’s presence). The Supreme Court and High Courts may take cognizance **suo** **motu**.
o Criminal contempt can also be initiated on a **motion by the Advocate General**, or—before the Supreme Court—by the **Attorney General or Solicitor General**. A **private individual may file such a motion** **only with the written consent** of the relevant law officer. For the Delhi High Court and certain Union Territories, a notified Law Officer’s consent is required.  **So, statement III is correct.**
● The Constitution does not lay down any fixed tenure, term of office, or retirement age for the Attorney General. The Attorney General serves **during the pleasure of the President**, meaning the President may remove the AG at any time, and the AG may also resign whenever they choose.   **So, statement IV is correct.**
o The **Constitution also does not specify the salary or emoluments** of the Attorney General.  Under Article 76(4), the Attorney General holds office during the **pleasure of the President** and **receives remuneration** **as** **determined by the President, not by Parliament.** There is no fixed tenure or salary prescribed in the Constitution; it is entirely at the discretion of the President. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
With reference to the transfer of Judges of the High
Courts in India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It may be effected either in the public interest
or by way of punishment.
II. 	 As part of the process, the Chief Justice of
India seeks the advice of a collegium made up of the four seniormost Judges of the Supreme Court.
III. 	A Public Interest Litigation (PIL) may be
instituted to challenge the transfer of a Judge.
Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **President** can transfer a **judge** from one **high court** to another after consulting the **Chief Justice of India** **(Article 222 of Constituion of India)**.
● On transfer, he/she is entitled to receive in addition to his/her **salary** such **compensatory allowance** as may be determined by **Parliament**.
● In **1977**, the **Supreme Court** ruled that the **transfer of high court judges** could be resorted to only as an **exceptional measure** and only in **public interest** and not by way of **punishment**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● In **1994**, the **Supreme Court** held that **judicial review** is necessary to check **arbitrariness** in **transfer of judges**. But, only the **judge** who is transferred can **challenge** it. **So, statement III is not correct.**
o In 2019 also, the **Madras High Court** held that only the **transferred judge** herself could challenge such an order, and even then, **only on limited grounds,** for instance, if the proposal for transfer had **not** **emanated from the Chief Justice of India (CJI)**. The judgment also reaffirmed that **consent of the judge** **is not required** for transfers initiated by the CJI.
● In the **Third Judges Case (1998)**, the **Supreme Court** opined that in case of the **transfer of high court judges**, the **Chief Justice of India** should consult, in addition to the **collegium of four seniormost judges of the Supreme** **Court**, the **chief justice of the two high courts** (one from which the judge is being transferred and the other receiving him/her). Thus, the **sole opinion of the Chief Justice of India** does not constitute the **‘consultation’** **process**. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Narco-Analysis in India,
consider the following statements:
I. - When conducted voluntarily and with procedural safeguards, the results of the test can be used as direct evidence in a court of law.
II. 	 The involuntary administration of such
tests infringe upon the Fundamental Rights guaranteed under Article 21.
III. 	 Facts or material evidence discovered as a
consequence of a voluntary test can be admissible under the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The practice of **Narco-analysis** which entails injecting a subject with **sodium pentothal** to induce a **semi-conscious** **state** has raised significant **constitutional and legal concerns** in India. The Supreme Court in **Selvi v. State of** **Karnataka (2010)** laid down **detailed guidelines** and declared **forced narco-tests unconstitutional,** emphasizing their impact on mental privacy, self-incrimination, and personal liberty.
● The Supreme Court in Selvi v. State of Karnataka (2010) held that the results of narco-analysis, polygraph, or brain mapping tests **cannot be admitted as direct evidence, even if conducted voluntarily.**  This is because statements made under the **influence of drugs** do not represent conscious and voluntary responses, thus failing to meet evidentiary standards under the **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA), 2023.** **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The involuntary administration of narco-analysis is a **violation of Article 21 of the Constitution of India,** which guarantees the **Right to Life and Personal Liberty,** including the right to mental privacy and dignity. In **D.K. Basu** **v. State of West Bengal (1997),** the Supreme Court further clarified that forcibly subjecting an individual to narcotests amounts to **cruel, inhuman, and degrading treatment.** Even voluntary tests must follow judicial and procedural safeguards to prevent coercion, as per **NHRC’s 2000 guidelines.** **So, statement II is correct.**
● As per  **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA), 2023,** any **material or fact discovered** on the basis of information revealed during a **voluntary narco-test can be admitted as evidence.** For instance, if the accused under a narcotest reveals the **location of a murder weapon** and it is subsequently recovered, the **recovery of that weapon** is admissible, though the statement itself is not. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Supreme Court recently ruled that forced or involuntary narco-analysis tests are unconstitutional and their results are inadmissible as evidence.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Indian Councils Act of 1909 introduced
indirect elections for the first time.
II. 	 The Government of India Act of 1919
expanded the system of separate electorates to include Sikhs.
III. 	The Government of India Act of 1935
introduced limited enfranchisement for women.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Morley-Minto Reforms** introduced several key provisions designed to expand Indian participation in governance**. The elected members** were to be **elected indirectly.** Local bodies were to elect an electoral college,
which would elect members of provincial legislatures, who would then elect members of the central legislature. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **The Government of India Act, 1919** (Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms) extended the system of separate electorates, initially introduced for Muslims in 1909**, to include Sikhs,** Indian Christians, Anglo-Indians, and Europeans.  **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms,** enacted through the **Government of India Act of 1919, introduced a** **limited franchise for women,** granting voting rights only to those who met certain qualifications, rather than extending suffrage to all women above the age of 21. This emphasizes that the enfranchisement was conditional and restricted. **So, statement III is not correct.**
o **The Government of India Act, 1935,** considerably **broadened the scope** of women’s limited enfranchisement, extending voting rights to a larger number of women based on qualifications such as property, education, or marital status, while still falling short of universal adult suffrage. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Central Vigilance Commission:
I. 	 It lacks the authority to independently order
the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI)  to investigate officers holding the rank of Joint Secretary or higher.
II. 	 It lacks the power to register criminal cases
and is confined to dealing with vigilance and disciplinary matters.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Central Vigilance Commission (CVC)** is an apex Indian governmental body created in **1964** to address governmental corruption. It was set up by the Government of India Resolution on February 11, 1964 on the recommendations of the **Committee on Prevention of Corruption, headed by Shri Santhanam.** In 2003, the **Parliament enacted a law conferring statutory status to the CVC.** It has the status of an autonomous body, free of control from any executive authority and is charged with monitoring all vigilance activities in the central government organisation.
● **The following are the limitations imposed on the Central Vigilance Commission (CVC):**
o It is **only an advisory body.** Central government departments may or may not pay heed to its advice. o It does not have adequate resources to cater to the number of complaints that it receives. It is a very small set-up with limited staff strength of around 299, as compared to more than 1500 central government establishments it is supposed to oversee. o **It cannot on its own direct CBI to initiate inquiries against any officer of the level of Joint Secretary** **and above.** The concerned department has to give permission for the same. **So, statement I is correct.** o It **does not** have the **power** to register **criminal cases**. It **can deal** only with **vigilance or disciplinary** **cases**. **So, statement II is correct.** o It has supervisory powers over CBI. However, **the Central Vigilance Commission Act, 2003, prohibits** **the CVC from issuing any directions to the DSPE,** i.e., CBI, to investigate or dispose of any case in a **particular manner.** CBI is under the administrative control of the **Department of Personnel and** **Training (DoPT),** which means that the powers to appoint, transfer, and suspend CBI officers lie with DoPT. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The CVC is a multi-member body **consisting of the Central Vigilance Commissioner** **(chairperson) and not more than two vigilance commissioners.**
● They are **appointed by the president** by warrant under his hand and seal on the **recommendation of a three-member committee consisting of the prime minister as its head,** **the Union minister of home affairs and the Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha.**
● They **hold office for a term of four years or until they attain the age of sixty-five years,** **whichever is earlier.**
● After their tenure, they are **not eligible for further employment** under the Central or a state government.
● **The president can remove the Central Vigilance Commissioner or any vigilance** **commissioner** from the office under the certain circumstances:
**Relevance:** The Vigilance Awareness Week 2025 is being observed across the country to promote probity in public life and achieve a corruption-free society.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 The President and the Governors enjoy
immunity from civil and criminal proceedings in respect of their personal acts during their tenure.
II. 	 As per the Constitution of India, Ministers are
protected from legal action for acts done in the course of their official duties.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **President and the Governor** enjoy several **privileges and immunities** under the Constitution. They possess **personal immunity** from **legal liability** for their **official acts**.
● During their **term of office**, they are **immune from any criminal proceedings**, even for **personal acts** committed before or after assuming office.
● They **cannot be arrested or imprisoned** by any court while in office. However, **civil proceedings** can be **instituted** **against them** during their term for acts done in their **personal capacity**, but only after giving a **two-month written** **notice** stating the **nature of the case**, **cause of action**, and **relief sought**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Constitution does not grant any immunity to ministers** for their **official acts**. However, unlike in **Britain**, Indian ministers are **not required to countersign** the official acts of the **President or the Governor**. Therefore, they are **not legally liable** in court for those acts. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Ministers are also **not liable for the official acts** done by the **President or the Governors** on their **advice**, since **courts are prohibited** from inquiring into the **nature of such advice**.
● However, ministers **do not enjoy any immunity** for their **personal acts**. They can be **sued or prosecuted** for **crimes and civil wrongs (torts)** in **ordinary courts**, just like any **common citizen**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
With regard to the legislative process in the State
Legislature, consider the following statements:
I. 	 If the President returns a Bill, the legislature
must undertake its reconsideration within six months.
II. 	 It is a constitutional obligation for the
President to give assent to a bill that has been reconsidered and passed by the State Legislature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● An **ordinary bill** in a state legislature can originate in **either House** in the case of a bicameral legislature. It can be introduced by **any minister or member**. The bill passes through **three stages** in the originating House: **first** **reading, second reading, and third reading**.
● After a **bill is passed** by the **originating House**, it is sent to the **second House,** where it undergoes the same **three-** **reading process**. If the Council passes it as is, or the Assembly accepts its amendments, the bill is treated as passed by both Houses and sent to the Governor. If the **Assembly disagrees** with the Council’s amendments, or if the **Council rejects or withholds the bill for three months**, the **Assembly may pass it again** and return it. Upon return, **if the Council rejects it**, proposes unacceptable changes, or does **not act** within **one month,** the bill is **deemed passed** in the form approved by the Assembly on its second passage.
● Thus, the **ultimate authority** in passing an ordinary bill lies with the **Legislative Assembly**, while the **Legislative** **Council can only delay** a bill for a **maximum of four months** (three months initially and one month later).
● When a **bill is reserved by the Governor** for the **consideration of the President**, if the **President returns the** **bill**, the **State Legislature** must **reconsider** it **within six months**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● After reconsideration, the bill is again **presented to the President** for assent, either **with or without amendments**. The **Constitution does not specify** whether it is **mandatory for the President to give assent to the bill after it** **has been reconsidered and passed again by the state legislature**. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About the Process Concerning the Passage of the Money Bill  in the State Legislature:**
● After being passed by the **Legislative Assembly**, the money bill is sent to the **Legislative Council** for its consideration. The **Legislative Council** has **limited powers** with respect to a Money Bill — it **cannot reject or amend** it. It can only **make recommendations** and must **return the bill within 14** **days**.
● The **Legislative Assembly** may either **accept or reject** all or any of the recommendations made by the Council. If the Assembly **accepts any recommendation**, the bill is deemed to have been passed by **both Houses** in the modified form. If the Assembly **does not accept any recommendation**, the bill is considered passed **in the original form** in which it was approved by the Assembly.
● If the **Legislative Council fails to return** the bill within **14 days**, it is deemed to have been **passed by** **both Houses** in the form originally passed by the **Legislative Assembly**. Hence, the **Legislative** **Assembly holds greater power** over the Council in the case of a Money Bill. The **maximum delay** the Council can cause is **14 days**.
● When a **Money Bill is presented to the Governor**, he or she may **give assent**, **withhold assent**, or **reserve the bill for the President’s consideration**, but **cannot return it** to the legislature for reconsideration. Generally, the **Governor gives assent**, since such a bill is introduced only with **prior** **permission**.
● If a **Money Bill is reserved for the President**, the **President may either give or withhold assent**, but **cannot return it** to the state legislature for reconsideration.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 A State Government can borrow only within
India on the security of the Consolidated Fund of the State.
II. 	 The discretionary grants under Article 282
are given to the States by the Centre on the recommendation of the Finance Commission.
III. 	A State cannot contract new loans without the
Centre’s consent if any previous Central loan or guarantee to that State remains outstanding.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constitution of India** lays down specific provisions regarding the **borrowing powers** of both the **Centre and** **the States** to maintain fiscal discipline and cooperative financial relations.
● The **Central Government** can borrow **within India or abroad** on the **security of the Consolidated Fund of India**. It can also give **guarantees**, but both are to be exercised **within limits fixed by Parliament**. However, **no such** **law** has yet been enacted by Parliament.
● A **State Government** can borrow **only within India** on the **security of the Consolidated Fund of the State** and can also give **guarantees**, but both within the **limits fixed by the State Legislature**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Central Government** can **make loans to any State** or **give guarantees** for loans raised by any State. The required funds for such loans are **charged on the Consolidated Fund of India**.
● A **State cannot raise any loan without the consent of the Centre** if there is still any **outstanding loan or** **guarantee** made earlier by the Centre to that State.  **So, statement III is correct.**
● **Article 282** of the Constitution deals with **discretionary grants** and empowers both the **Union** and **State** **Governments** to make **grants for any public purpose**, irrespective of whether the subject of the grant is included in the **Union List, State List, or Concurrent List** of the **Seventh Schedule**.
● These grants can be given **even for purposes beyond the legislative competence** of the granting government, as long as they serve a **public purpose**.
● The grants under Article 282 can be **conditional or unconditional,** depending on the discretion of the Union or the State Government. They **do not fall under the purview of the Finance Commission’s recommendations**, meaning they are **outside the constitutional mechanism** governing statutory transfers.  **So, statement II is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I** The Supreme Court of India is regarded as a Court of Record. **Statement-II** Its judgments and proceedings are preserved as legal precedents. **Statement-III** It has the power to punish for contempt of itself and of subordinate courts.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are
correct and both of them explain Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are
correct, but only one of them explains Statement–I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements–II and III is
correct and that explains Statement–I
options.d:
Neither Statement–II nor Statement–III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 129** of the Constitution of India states that the **Supreme Court shall be a court of record** and shall have **all the powers of such a court**, including the **power to punish for contempt of itself**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **judgements, proceedings, and acts** of the Supreme Court are recorded for **perpetual memory and** **testimony**.
o These records are **admitted as evidence** and **cannot be questioned** when produced before any court. They are also **recognised as legal precedents and authoritative legal references**.  **So, statement II is correct.** o The Supreme Court has the **power to punish for contempt of itself**. In **1991**, the Court ruled that it can punish for **contempt not only of itself but also of High Courts, subordinate courts, and tribunals** functioning across the country. **So, statement III is correct.** **So, both statement–II and statement–III are correct and both of them explain statement–I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Contempt of Courts:**
● To define and regulate the law relating to contempt, the **Government of India** in **1961** appointed the **H.N. Sanyal Committee**, headed by the then **Additional Solicitor General of India**, to review the existing framework.
● The committee submitted its report in **1963**, and based on its recommendations, the **Contempt of Courts** **Act, 1971** was enacted by **Parliament**.
● **There are two types of Contempt:** o **Civil Contempt:** Wilful disobedience to any **judgment, order, writ, or process of a court**, or **wilful breach of an undertaking** given to a court. o **Criminal Contempt:** The publication of any matter or act which — (i) **Scandalises or lowers** the authority of a court, or (ii) **Prejudices or interferes** with the due course of a judicial proceeding, or (iii) **Interferes or obstructs** the administration of justice in any manner.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Zonal Councils, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 All of them are established under Article 263
of the Constitution of India.
II. 	 The secretariat looking after all Zonal
Councils is located in New Delhi under the administrative control of the Ministry of Home Affairs.
III. 	Each Zonal Council has a Standing Committee
consisting of Chief Secretaries of the member States.
IV. 	 The North Eastern States are not included in
the Zonal Councils.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Zonal Councils** are **statutory** (and not **constitutional**) bodies. They were established by the **States** **Reorganisation Act, 1956**. The Act divided the country into **five zones** — **Northern, Central, Eastern, Western,** **and Southern** — and provided a **Zonal Council** for each zone. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Secretariat** for all Zonal Councils is **based in New Delhi** and functions under the administrative control of the **Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA).**  **So, statement II is correct.**
● Each **Zonal Council** consists of the **Union Home Minister (as Chairman),** **Chief Ministers of all states** in the zone, **two other ministers from each state**, and the **Administrators of Union Territories** in the zone.
o The **Union Home Minister** serves as the **common Chairman** of all five councils, while the **Chief** **Ministers** act as **Vice-Chairmen by rotation** for one year at a time.
● The following are **advisory members** (without voting rights): one **nominee of NITI Aayog**, the **Chief Secretary** of each state, and the **Development Commissioner** of each state in the zone.
● Each Zonal Council has a **Standing Committee** made up of the **Chief Secretaries** of the member states of that zone.  **So, statement III is correct.**
● The **North-Eastern States** are not part of the five Zonal Councils. Their issues instead fall under the **North Eastern** **Council (NEC).**  **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
questions.questionText:
With reference to the decision-making process
in the GST Council, consider the following statements:
I. 	 For any meeting of the Council, one-half of
its total membership constitutes the quorum.
II. 	 A decision requires at least three-fourths of
the weighted votes of members present and voting.
III. 	The Central Government’s vote carries two-
thirds weightage while the states together carry one-third weightage.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The 101st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2016 introduced the Goods and Services Tax (GST) and mandated cooperative fiscal federalism between the Centre and the states. To enable this, it inserted **Article 279A**, empowering the President to establish the **GST Council**, which was constituted in 2016. The Council’s Secretariat is based in New Delhi, and the Union Revenue Secretary serves as its **ex-officio Secretary**.
● The quorum for a GST Council meeting is **one-half of the total number of members**.  **So, statement I is correct.**
● Every decision of the Council must be taken by a majority of **not less than three-fourths of the weighted votes** of members present and voting.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Constitution provides that:
o The **Central Government’s vote has a weightage of one-third**, and o The **combined votes of all State Governments have a weightage of two-thirds**. **So, statement III is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Functions of the Council**
● The Council is required to make recommendations to the centre and the states on the following matters:
o The taxes, cesses and surcharges levied by the centre, the states and the local bodies that would get merged in GST. o The goods and services that may be subjected to GST or exempted from GST. o Model GST Laws, principles of levy, apportionment of GST levied on supplies in the course of interstate trade or commerce and the principles that govern the place of supply. o The threshold limit of turnover below which goods and services may be exempted from GST. o The rates including floor rates with bands of GST. o Any special rate or rates for a specified period to raise additional resources during any natural calamity or disaster. o Special provision with respect to the states of Arunachal Pradesh, Assam, Jammu and Kashmir4 , Manipur, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Nagaland, Sikkim, Tripura, Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand. o Any other matter relating to GST, as the Council may decide.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the 105th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2021:
I. 	 It empowered Parliament to identify and
specify Socially and Educationally Backward Classes (SEBCs) for both the Central and State Lists.
II. 	 Any addition to or removal from the Central
List of SEBCs can be effected only through a Presidential notification.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Under **Article 342A(1),** the **President**, after consulting the **Governor** of a State, can by **public notification** specify the **socially and educationally backward classes** in the **Central List** for that State or Union territory. This list applies **only for Central Government purposes**.
● The term **“Central List”** means the list of socially and educationally backward classes prepared and maintained by the Central Government.
● The Constitution did not originally specify which citizens would be considered **Socially and Educationally** **Backward Classes (SEBCs)** or **Other Backward Classes (OBCs)**. The **102nd Constitutional Amendment Act,** **2018** empowered the **President** to specify the **SEBCs** for each state and union territory.
● The 105th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2021 further modified this provision. It stated that the **President may** **specify the SEBCs in the Central List**, which would apply to the **Central Government** for that state or union territory. The President issues this notification after **consultation with the Governor**, but any **inclusion or**
**exclusion** in the **Central List** can be done **only by Parliament**, not through another Presidential notification.  **So,** **statements I and II are not correct.**
● The 105th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2021also clarified that the term **“Central List”** means the list of socially and educationally backward classes **prepared and maintained by the Central Government**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.sectionGroup: Fiscal Policy, Budget, Taxation
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Tax Appropriated by
I. - Taxes on agricultural income
Centre
II. - Excise duty on alcoholic liquor for human consumption
State
III. - Taxes on the sale of petroleum crude
Centre and State
IV. - Taxes on mineral rights State
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constitution of India** provides a detailed framework for the **distribution of tax revenues** between the Centre and the States under various Articles (268–271).
● Under **Article 270**, all other Union taxes and duties except those under Articles 268, 269, 269-A, surcharges under Article 271, and specific-purpose cesses are distributed between the Centre and the States. The Finance Commission recommends how these net proceeds are to be shared.
● **Article 271** empowers Parliament to **levy surcharges** on certain central taxes and duties, the **proceeds of which** **belong exclusively to the Centre**. However, **surcharges cannot be imposed on GST**.
● Finally, **taxes levied, collected, and retained by the States** (18 in number) are listed in the **State List (List II)**. These include land revenue, **taxes on agricultural income, mineral rights, alcoholic liquor for human** **consumption,** taxes on electricity, **taxes on the sale of petroleum crude**, vehicles, professions, entertainments, and stamp duties on non-Union documents. These taxes form the **exclusive revenue domain of the States**, ensuring fiscal autonomy within the federal framework. **So, pairs II & IV are correctly matched and pairs I and III are** **not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Distribution of Taxes:**
● Under **Article 268**, certain taxes are **levied by the Centre but collected and appropriated by** **the States**, such as **stamp duties on bills of exchange, cheques, insurance policies, and** **transfer of shares**. The proceeds from these taxes belong entirely to the state where they are collected.
● According to **Article 269**, some taxes are **levied and collected by the Centre but assigned to** **the States**. These include **taxes on the sale or purchase of goods** and **consignment of goods in** **the course of inter-state trade or commerce**. The net proceeds of these taxes are distributed among states according to principles determined by Parliament.
● **Article 269-A** provides for the **levy and collection of GST on inter-state trade or commerce** by the Centre, with the **proceeds shared between the Centre and the States** as per the recommendations of the GST Council. Parliament is also empowered to determine the **place of** **supply** for GST purposes.
● The **distribution of tax revenues** in India underwent major reforms through the **80th** **Amendment Act, 2000** and the **101st Amendment Act, 2016**.
● The 80th Amendment was enacted to implement the recommendations of the **10th Finance** **Commission**, which introduced the **Alternative Scheme of Devolution**.
o The Commission proposed that the entirety of central tax revenues should be subject to sharing with the states.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following scenarios is least
likely to amount to a violation of personality rights in India?

options.a:
Unauthorized use of a celebrity's distinctive
voice in a television advertisement for commercial gain
options.b:
Creation of a satirical caricature imitating the
mannerisms of a well-known political figure in an editorial cartoon
options.c:
Commercial sale of merchandise bearing the
image of a film actor without prior consent
options.d:
Publication of a fictitious commercial
endorsement attributed to a prominent sportsperson in a newspaper advertisement

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In India, **personality rights** protect an **individual’s name, image, likeness, voice, and other personal attributes** from **unauthorized commercial exploitation.**
● Courts have recognized these rights as part of the **right to privacy and dignity under Article 21 of Constitution** **of India**, supported by intellectual property laws such as the **Copyright Act, 1957 and Trade Marks Act, 1999.** However, this protection is **not absolute** and must be balanced with **freedom of speech and expression under** **Article 19(1)(a).**
● Judicial precedents, including **DM Entertainment v. Baby Gift House (2010)** and **Digital Collectibles v. Galactus** **(2023),** clarify that **parody, satire, caricature, and criticism of public figures** are **legitimate** forms of expression. Hence, a **satirical caricature of a political figure is least likely to violate** personality rights, unlike commercial misuses for profit. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Delhi High Court has recently expanded the protection of personality rights for Bollywood stars against unauthorised use of their images, voices, and likenesses.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Interim Government of
1946, consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Viceroy retained the right to veto
legislative proposals.
II. 	 The Prime Minister functioned as the head of
the Executive Council.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Interim Government** was established as a temporary administration bridging the imperial structure and a democratic framework. **It functioned from August 1946 until India’s independence on August 15, 1947**, when the subcontinent was partitioned into India and Pakistan.
● Its formation came from the newly elected Constituent Assembly, whose representatives were indirectly chosen by provincial legislatures. In these elections, the Indian National Congress (INC) won a plurality with 208 seats (about 69%), while the Muslim League secured 73 seats.
● The Interim Government was not a new, independent body but a **reconstitution of the Viceroy's Executive** **Council.** The **members** of the interim Government were **members of the Viceroy’s Executive Council.**
● The **Viceroy**, who also held the title of **Governor-General,** was the formal President and **Head of the Executive** **Council. So, statement II is not correct.**
o **Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru served as its Vice President and de facto Prime Minister.** o Although initially opposed to joining the Interim Government, the Muslim League eventually participated to gain political leverage.
● Although the Interim Government held significant administrative power, **it was not fully sovereign.** The **Viceroy** (Lord Wavell, followed by Lord Mountbatten) retained **supreme authority** under the existing Government of India Act 1935. This included **an absolute veto power,** allowing him to overrule any decision or legislation passed by the council. **So, statement I is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Interim Government (1946)**
● **First Cabinet of Free India (1947)**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the administration of the Union Territories:
I. 	 Parliament can make laws on any subject in
all three lists under the Seventh Schedule for Union Territories.
II. 	 The Constitution of India contains separate
provisions for the administration of territories that can be acquired in future.
III. 	The Administrator of a Union Territory
functions as the head of the State like a Governor.
Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Articles 239 to 241 in Part VIII of the Constitution of India** deal with the Union Territories. Even though all the Union Territories belong to one category, there is no uniformity in their administrative system. Every Union Territory is administered by the President acting through an **administrator appointed by him. An administrator** **of a Union Territory is an agent of the President and not the head of State like a Governor.  So, statement III** **is not correct.**
● **Parliament can make laws on any subject in all three lists under the Seventh Schedule,** **including the State** **List, for Union Territories**. This power extends to Puducherry, Delhi and Jammu & Kashmir, even though they have their own legislatures. However, their assemblies can also legislate on:
o Puducherry: State and Concurrent Lists. o Delhi: State (except public order, police and land) and Concurrent Lists. o Jammu & Kashmir: State (except public order and police) and Concurrent Lists. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The President can make regulations for the peace, progress and good government of the **Andaman and Nicobar** **Islands, Lakshadweep, Dadra and Nagar Haveli, Daman and Diu and Ladakh and not all Union Territories.** In the case of Puducherry, also, the President can also legislate by making regulations but only when the assembly is suspended or dissolved. A regulation made by the President has the same force and effect as an act of Parliament and can also repeal or amend any act of Parliament in relation to these Union Territories.
● The **Constitution of India does not contain any separate provisions for the administration of acquired** **territories.** But the constitutional provisions for the administration of Union Territories also apply to the acquired territories. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the suspension of Fundamental Rights during a National Emergency in India:
I. 	 The Fundamental Rights under Article 19
are automatically suspended in case of both External Emergency as well as Internal Emergency.
II. 	 Under Article 359, the suspension of
enforcement of rights is not automatic and requires a Presidential Order specifying
which Fundamental Rights are suspended.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **suspension of Fundamental Rights** during a **National Emergency** in India is governed by **Articles 358 and** **359** of the Constitution. Both provisions empower the State to limit certain rights temporarily **to ensure national** **security** and effective governance during extraordinary circumstances.
● **Article 358** is confined to Fundamental Rights under **Article 19** of Constitution of India (Protection of certain rights regarding freedom of speech, etc.), while **Article 359** empowers the **President** to **suspend the right to move any** **court** for the enforcement of any Fundamental Rights during a National Emergency, either throughout India or in part of it.  However, the **44th Amendment Act (1978)** introduced a crucial safeguard — it **prohibits the President**
**from suspending the right to seek judicial enforcement of Articles 20 and 21, even during an emergency** **(external or internal).**
o **Article 358 operates only in case of External Emergency** (that is, when the emergency is declared on the grounds of war or external aggression) and not in the case of Internal Emergency (ie, when the Emergency is declared on the grounds of armed rebellion). **Article 359, on the other hand, operates in case of both** **External Emergency as well as Internal Emergency.**
● The **automatic suspension of Article 19** occurs only under **Article 358.** Under **Article 359,** the suspension of enforcement of rights is **not automatic** — it requires a specific **Presidential Order** specifying which Fundamental Rights are suspended. **So, statement I is not correct and statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part II: Citizenship
questions.questionText:
Consider the following situations:
I. 	 If she/he has unlawfully traded or communicated with the enemy during a war
II. 	 If she/he has voluntarily acquired the
citizenship of another country during a war, with which India is engaged
III. 	If she/he has been ordinarily resident outside
India for seven years continuously In how many of the above circumstances can the Central Government deprive a person of Indian Citizenship?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Constitution of India deals with citizenship from **Articles 5 to 11 under Part II.** However, it contains neither any permanent nor any elaborate provisions in this regard. It only identifies the persons who became citizens of India at its commencement (i.e., on January 26, 1950).
● **The Citizenship Act, 1955** prescribes three ways of losing citizenship whether acquired under the Act or prior to it under the Constitution, viz, renunciation, termination and deprivation:
● **Deprivation** is a compulsory termination of Indian citizenship by the Central Government, if:
o the citizen has obtained citizenship by fraud; o the citizen has shown disloyalty to the Constitution of India; o the citizen **has unlawfully traded or communicated with the enemy during a war**. **So, point I is correct.** o the citizen has, within five years after registration or naturalisation, been imprisoned in any country for two years; and o The citizen has been ordinarily resident outside India for seven years continuously. **So, point III is correct.**
▪ This will not apply if s/he is a student abroad, or is in the service of a Government in India or an international organisation of which India is a member, or has registered annually at an Indian consulate with her/his intention to retain her/his Indian citizenship.
● **By Termination:** When an Indian citizen voluntarily (consciously, knowingly and without duress, undue influence or compulsion) acquires the citizenship of another country, his Indian citizenship automatically terminates. **This** **provision, however, does not apply during a war in which India is engaged**. **So, point II is not correct.** **So, in only two of the circumstances can the Central Government deprive a person of Indian Citizenship.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I** The Finance Commission is a quasi-judicial body. **Statement-II** The Finance Commission can summon witnesses, examine records, and require the production of documents in connection with its recommendations. **Statement-III** The recommendations of the Finance Commission carry the same weight as a decree of a civil court.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are
correct and both of them explain Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–II and Statement–III are
correct, but only one of them explains Statement–I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements–II and III is
correct and that explains Statement–I
options.d:
Neither Statement–II nor Statement–III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Quasi-judicial bodies are non-judicial institutions** vested with the **authority to interpret and apply the law,** such as arbitration panels or tribunals, and are conferred with powers and procedural mechanisms analogous to those exercised by courts or judicial officers.
● **Article 280** of the Constitution of India provides for a **Finance Commission as a quasi-judicial body.** It is constituted by the President of India every fifth year or at such earlier time as he considers necessary. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● The Commission may regulate its own procedure and, while performing its functions, **shall exercise all powers of** **a civil court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908,** in matters such as:
o **Summoning and enforcing the attendance of witnesses;** o **Requiring the production of documents;** o **Requisitioning public records from any court or office**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Finance Commission **does have certain powers of a civil court**, such as summoning witnesses or requiring the production of documents. But these procedural powers **do not** make its **recommendations** equivalent to **a civil** **court decree.** A civil court decree is **binding and enforceable**, whereas Finance Commission recommendations are **purely advisory** under Article 280. **So, only one of the Statements–II and III is correct and that explains Statement–I**
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the government extended the tenure of the 16th Finance Commission by one month to submit its award for tax devolution and grants for the next five year.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I** If a State refuses to implement the Centre’s directive on the three-language formula, then the President’s Rule may be imposed in that State. **Statement-II** Article 365 allows the President to impose President's Rule if a State fails to comply with the directions given by the Centre.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct
and Statement–II explains Statement–I
options.b:
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are
correct, but Statement–II does **not** explain Statement–I
options.c:
Statement–I is correct, but Statement–II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **President’s Rule can be proclaimed under Article 356** on **two grounds–one mentioned in Article 356 itself** **and another in Article 365:**
o **Article 356** empowers the President to issue a proclamation if **he is satisfied that a situation has arisen** **in which the government of a state cannot be carried on in accordance with the provisions of the** **Constitution.** o **Article 365** says that **whenever a State fails to comply with or to give effect to any direction from the** **Centre,** it will be lawful for the President to hold that a situation has arisen in which the Government of the State cannot be carried on in accordance with the provisions of the Constitution. In such a situation, the President’s Rule can be imposed. **So, statement II is correct.** o **Thus, a State’s refusal** to implement the Union government’s directive on the **three-language formula** can be a ground for imposing **President’s Rule. So, statement I is correct.**
● In the **Bommai Case (1994),** the Supreme Court listed the situations where the exercise of power under Article 356 could be proper or improper. The imposition of President’s Rule in a State would be proper in the following situations:
o When, after general elections to the assembly, no party secures a majority. o When the party having a majority in the assembly declines to form a ministry, and the governor cannot find a coalition ministry. o When a ministry resigns after its defeat in the assembly and no other party is willing or able to form a ministry commanding a majority in the assembly. o **When a constitutional direction of the Central government is disregarded by the State Government.** o Internal subversion: for example, when a government is deliberately acting against the Constitution. o Physical breakdown: when the government wilfully refuses to discharge its constitutional obligations, endangering the security of the state.
● A proclamation imposing President’s Rule must be approved by both the Houses of Parliament within two months from the date of its issue. If approved by both the Houses of Parliament, the President’s Rule continues for six months. **It can be extended for a maximum period of three years with the approval of the Parliament, every** **six months**.
o However, the **President’s Rule** can be **extended** **beyond the** period of **one year** only if a **proclamation** **of National Emergency** is in operation in any part of the State, and the **Election Commission certifies** that the general elections to the legislative assembly of the concerned State cannot be held on account of difficulties. **So, both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct and Statement–II explains Statement–I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with respect to
the Prorogation of the House of Parliament:
I. 	 The presiding officer can prorogue the House
