while in session.
II. 	 It does not affect the bills pending before the
House.
III. 	It is done on the advice of the Council of
Ministers.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Prorogation** refers to the **formal end of a session of Parliament**, not just a single sitting. It is **carried out by the** **President, not by the presiding officer.** Under **Article 85(2)(a)** of the Constitution, the President has the authority to prorogue either House, acting on the advice of the Council of Ministers. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Adjournment ends a sitting** (daily meeting), while **prorogation ends an entire session** of Parliament.
● **Article 85** gives the **President** the authority to **summon or prorogue either House of Parliament and to dissolve** **the Lok Sabha** on the **advice of the Council of Ministers**. Prorogation generally takes place after the House has been adjourned sine die, though the President may prorogue it even while in session. This action ends the session and temporarily halts parliamentary work. **So, statement III is correct.**
● **Prorogation does not cancel any pending bills or motions; all unfinished business can continue in the next** **session**. However, all pending notices (other than those for introducing bills) lapse on prorogation and fresh notices have to be given for the next session. This differs from dissolution, which ends the tenure of the Lok Sabha and causes all pending bills to lapse, except those still under consideration in the Rajya Sabha. **So, statement II is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
With reference to the State Legislative Council,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 The qualifying age for membership in the
Rajya Sabha and the Legislative Council is the same.
II. 	 A person nominated by the Governor to the
Legislative Council must be an elector for an Assembly constituency in the respective State.
III. 	If the Chief Minister is a member of the
Legislative Council, he/she becomes responsible to both Houses of the State Legislature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India follows a **bicameral system** at both the **Central and State levels**. Under this system, the **State Legislature** may consist of two Houses — the **Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha)** and the **Legislative Council (Vidhan** **Parishad)**.
● The Constitution of India **does not mandate** states to form a Legislative Council. As of **January 2020**, only **six** **states** have a **bicameral legislature** — Andhra Pradesh, Bihar, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Telangana, and Uttar Pradesh.
● To become a **member of the Legislative Council** and the **Rajya Sabha (Council of States)**, a person must be a **citizen of India** and must have **attained the age of 30 years**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Under the **Representation of the People Act, 1951**, a person can be **elected** to the Legislative Council only if they are an **elector for an Assembly constituency** in the concerned state, and to be **nominated by the Governor**, they must be a **resident of that state**.  **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The **Council of Ministers, including the Chief Minister,** is collectively responsible **only to the Legislative** **Assembly (Vidhan Sabha). So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About State Legislative Council:**
● There is **no uniformity** in the organisation of State Legislatures. Most states have a **unicameral system**, while a few have a **bicameral system**. Currently, **six states**, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, Maharashtra, and Karnataka, have **two Houses**.
● Under **Article 169**, the **Parliament** has the power to **create or abolish** a Legislative Council in a State. This can be done if the Legislative Assembly of that State passes a resolution to that effect by a **special** **majority,** that is, a majority of the total membership of the assembly and not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting.
● Such an **Act of Parliament** to create or abolish a Legislative Council is **not considered a constitutional** **amendment** under Article 368, but is passed like an ordinary law by a simple majority.
● Members of the **Legislative Assembly** are **directly elected** by the people through elections, while the **Legislative Council** is a **permanent body** that can be **created or abolished** when the Legislative Assembly passes a **special resolution** under **Article 169** of the Constitution.
● The **term** of the Legislative Assembly is **five years**, unless dissolved earlier, while the **tenure** of the members of the Legislative Council is **six years**, with **one-third of the members retiring every two** **years**.
● The **Legislative Council** has **limited powers**. It **cannot form or dissolve a government** and **cannot** **reject a Finance Bill**.
● Although it is a **permanent body**, the **Legislative Council can be dissolved** if Parliament passes a law to that effect after the concerned State Legislative Assembly passes a **special majority resolution**. For instance, the **Andhra Pradesh Legislative Council** was **abolished in 1985** but **reinstated in 2007**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Constitution of India specifies the official
languages of different States.
II. 	 The President of India may direct a language
to be officially recognized in a state if a substantial part of the population so desires.
Which of the statements given above is/are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Articles 343 to 351** of the Constitution deal with the **official language** and are divided into four categories: Language of the Union, Regional Languages, Language of the Judiciary and Texts of Laws, and Special Directives.
● The Constitution of India **does not specify** the **official language of individual states. So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● Constitution of India provides that:
o A state legislature may adopt any one or more languages in use in the state, or Hindi, as its official language. o Until such adoption, English remains the official language of the state. English also serves as the link language for communication between the Union and states, or between states, though two or more states may mutually agree to use Hindi instead. o The Official Languages Act, 1963 mandates that English be used for communication with non-Hindi states, and any Hindi communication with non-Hindi states must include an English translation.
● If a **substantial portion of a state’s population requests** official recognition of their language, t**he President may** **direct** that it be officially recognized, protecting the linguistic interests of minorities. **statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements best
implies that the Fundamental Rights promote the idea of political democracy in India?

options.a:
They are most essential for the all-round
development of the citizens.
options.b:
They empower citizens to engage in electoral
democracy through the right to vote.
options.c:
They are fundamental in governance and
serve as a crucial test for the performance of the Government.
options.d:
They operate as limitations on the tyranny
of the executive and arbitrary laws of the legislature.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Part III of the Constitution of India guarantees six **Fundamental Rights** to all the citizens: Right to Equality, Right to Freedom, Right against Exploitation, Right to Freedom of Religion, Cultural and Educational Rights and Right to Constitutional Remedies.
● The Fundamental Rights are meant for **promoting the idea of political democracy**. They **operate as limitations** **on the tyranny of the executive and the arbitrary laws** of the Legislature.
o They are justiciable in nature, that is, they are enforceable by the courts for their violation. o The aggrieved person can directly go to the Supreme Court which can issue the writs of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, certiorari and quo warranto for the restoration of his rights. o They prevent the establishment of an authoritarian and despotic rule in the country, and protect the liberties and freedoms of the people against the invasion by the State. o In short, they aim at establishing ‘a government of laws and not of men’.
● **Fundamental Rights** are so named because they are guaranteed by the Constitution and are **essential for the all-** **round material, intellectual, moral, and spiritual development of individuals.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following features of Constitution of
India:
I. 	 Rule of Law
II. 	 Prerogative writs
III. 	Nomination of members to the Rajya Sabha
IV. 	 Procedure for amendment of the Constitution
V.  	 Vesting of residuary powers in the Centre
How many of the above features were adopted from the British Constitution?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Constitution of India** has borrowed most of its provisions from the Constitutions of various other countries as well as from the Government of India Act, 1935. Dr. B.R. Ambedkar proudly claimed that the Constitution of India was framed after ransacking all the known Constitutions of the World.
**Sources** **Features Borrowed**
Government of India Act of 1935
Federal Scheme, Office of the Governor, Judiciary, Public Service Commissions, Emergency provisions and administrative details
**British Constitution** Parliamentary government, **Rule of Law**, legislative procedure, single citizenship, cabinet system, **prerogative writs**, parliamentary privileges and bicameralism. **So, points I and II are correct.**
US Constitution Fundamental rights, independence of the judiciary, judicial review, impeachment of the president, removal of Supreme Court and high court judges and the post of Vice President
**Irish Constitution** Directive Principles of State Policy, **nomination of members to the Rajya** **Sabha** and the method of election of the President. **So, point III is not correct.**
**Canadian** **Constitution**
Federation with a strong Centre, **vesting of residuary powers in the Centre,** appointment of state governors by the Centre, and advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court.  **So, point V is not correct.**
Australian Constitution
Concurrent List, freedom of trade, commerce and inter commerce, and joint sitting of the two Houses of Parliament
Weimar Constitution of Germany
Suspension of Fundamental Rights during Emergency.
Soviet Constitution (USSR, now Russia)
Fundamental duties and the ideal of justice (social, economic and political) in the Preamble
French Constitution Republic and the ideals of liberty, equality and fraternity in the Preamble
**South** **African** **Constitution**
**Procedure for amendment of the Constitution** and election of members of the Rajya Sabha. So, pair II is not correctly matched.  **So, point IV is not** **correct.**
Japanese Constitution Procedure established by Law
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
questions.questionText:
“The edifice of our Constitution is based upon the
basic elements mentioned in the Preamble. If any of these elements are removed, the structure will not survive and it will not be the same Constitution, or it cannot maintain its identity.” In which one of the following cases did the Supreme Court of India made the above observation?

options.a:
Kesavananda Bharati vs. State of Kerala case
(1973)
options.b:
Re: The Berubari Union and Exchange of
Enclaves Case (1960)
options.c:
Minerva Mills Ltd. and Ors. vs. Union Of
India and Ors. (1980)
options.d:
I. C. Golaknath & Ors. vs. State of Punjab &
Anrs (1967)

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Kesavananda Bharati case** is a landmark judgment balancing individual rights with Parliament's power to amend the Constitution. The case was heard by a 13-judge bench. The Court ruled that while Parliament has broad powers to amend the Constitution under Article 368, it cannot alter or destroy its basic structure. This ruling effectively established what is now known as the **basic structure doctrine**, which serves as a safeguard against potential legislative overreach.
o The court held that the **Preamble is a part of the Constitution** and the opinion tendered by it in the **Berubari Union (1960) in this regard** **was wrong**, and held that the Preamble can be amended, subject to the condition that no amendment is made to the ‘basic features’. **So, option (a) is correct.**
● In **1960**, the Supreme Court, in the **Berubari Union Case**, examined the 1958 Nehru-Noon Agreement on dividing Berubari between India and Pakistan. The Court held that **cession of Indian territory requires a constitutional** **amendment under Article 368**, as ordinary legislation under Article 3 is insufficient. It clarified that such amendments need a **special majority in both Houses**, ensuring democratic accountability. The Court noted the **Preamble is only a guiding framework** and does not empower Parliament to cede territory.  **So, option (b) is not** **correct.**
● In **Minerva Mills vs. Union of India (1980)**, the Court **reaffirmed** the **basic structure doctrine**, holding that Parliament cannot amend the Constitution in a way that alters its essential features. Provisions of the **42nd** **Amendment** giving priority to Directive Principles over Fundamental Rights were struck down**. So, option (c) is** **not correct.**
● In **Golaknath vs. State of Punjab (1967)**, by a 6:5 majority, the Court ruled that Parliament **cannot amend** **Fundamental Rights** under Article 368 in a manner violating them, and all such amendments are subject to judicial review. This judgment strengthened civil liberties and laid the foundation for the **Kesavananda Bharati (1973)** **case**. **So, option (d) is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.questionText:
With reference to Article 323A of the Constitution
of India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It provides for the establishment of tribunals
only for matters related to public services.
II. 	 It provides for a multi-tiered system of tribunals
to adjudicate the matters referred to them.
III. 	It excludes the jurisdiction of High Courts
over matters assigned to these tribunals.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 323A** of the Constitution empowers Parliament to establish **administrative tribunals** to adjudicate disputes related to recruitment and conditions of service of persons in public services of the Centre, states, local bodies, public corporations, and other public authorities. It aims to provide a **specialized forum** for speedy and expert resolution of service-related disputes.
● **Article 323A** empowers Parliament to establish administrative tribunals for **public service matters only**, including recruitment and service conditions of persons in the Centre, states, local bodies, public corporations, and other public authorities. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Article 323A** provides for tribunals for the Centre and each state, but **does not envisage a hierarchy of tribunals.** Hierarchies are allowed under Article 323B, which deals with tribunals for other matters like taxation, land reforms, and other specified subjects. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Originally, Article 323A **excluded the jurisdiction of High Courts**, but in the **Chandra Kumar v. Union of India** **(1997)** case, the Supreme Court held that such exclusion was **unconstitutional**, thereby restoring judicial review against tribunal orders. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Under **Article 323 B**, the Parliament and the state legislatures are authorised to provide for the establishment of tribunals for the adjudication of disputes relating to the following matters:
o Taxation o Foreign exchange, import and export o Industrial and labour o Land reforms o Ceiling on urban property o Elections to Parliament and state legislatures o Food stuffs o Rent and tenancy rights

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part I: The Union and its territory
questions.microTopic: Union, States and their Territory, UTs
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
State/Union Territory (UT)
Status immediately before attaining statehood or UT status
I. - Puducherry Acquired territory
II. - Meghalaya Autonomous state
III. - Sikkim Protectorate state
IV. - Daman
and Diu
Associate state
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● After the large-scale reorganisation of the States in 1956, the political map of India underwent continuous changes due to the pressure of popular agitations and political conditions.
● **Puducherry:** The territory of Puducherry comprises the former French establishments in India known as Puducherry, Karaikal, Mahe and Yanam. The French handed over this territory to India in 1954. Subsequently, it was administered as an ‘**acquired territory**’, **till 1962, when it was made a UT** by the 14th Constitutional Amendment Act. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **In 1972, Manipur, Tripura, and Meghalaya** attained statehood, while Mizoram and Arunachal Pradesh (then NEFA) became Union Territories, raising India’s states to 21 (Manipur 19th, Tripura 20th, Meghalaya 21st). Initially, **the 22nd Amendment (1969)** had created **Meghalaya as an autonomous state** within Assam**,** but this did not satisfy local aspirations. Mizoram and Arunachal Pradesh were also carved out of Assam**. So, pair II is** **correctly matched.**
● Until 1947, **Sikkim was a princely** state ruled by the Chogyal. After becoming an **Indian protectorate**, the 35th Amendment (1974) made it **an associate state.** Following a 1975 referendum abolishing the Chogyal, the **36th** **Amendment (1975)** granted Sikkim **full statehood** as India’s 22nd state. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **Goa, Daman and Diu:** India acquired these three territories from the Portuguese by means of a police action in 1961. They were constituted as a **Union Territory (UT) by the 12th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1962.** Later, in 1987, Goa was conferred statehood. Consequently, Daman and Diu were made a separate UT.  **So, pair IV is** **not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the State Public Service Commission (SPSC):
I. 	 The chairperson and members of an SPSC
can be removed by the President only.
II. 	 The chairperson and members of an SPSC are
not eligible for a second term.
III. 	The Governor can exclude certain posts and
services from the SPSC’s jurisdiction.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **State Public Service Commission (SPSC)** is a **constitutional body** established under **Articles 315 to 323 of** **the Constitution of India.** It is responsible for conducting examinations and recruiting candidates for various state civil services, ensuring a fair and merit-based selection process.
● A State Public Service Commission consists of a **chairman and other members appointed by the Governor of** **the State.** Although the chairman and members of an SPSC are appointed by the Governor, they **can be removed** **only by the President** (and not by the Governor). The President can remove them on the **same grounds and in the** **same manner** as he can remove a **chairman or a member of the Union Public Service Commission**. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The **chairman of a SPSC** (on ceasing to hold office) is eligible for appointment as the **chairman or a member of** **the UPSC** or as the **chairman of any other SPSC,** but **not for any other employment under the Government** **of India or a State.** A **member** of a SPSC (on ceasing to hold office) is **eligible** for appointment as the **chairman** **or a member of the UPSC, or as the chairman of that SPSC or any other SPSC**, but not for any other employment under the Government of India or a State. **The chairman or a member of a SPSC is (after having** **completed his first term) not eligible for reappointment to that office (that is, not eligible for a second term).** **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Governor of the concerned State can exclude posts, services and matters from the purview of the SPSC.** The Constitution states that the Governor, in respect to the State services and posts, may make regulations specifying the matters in which it shall not be necessary for SPSC to be consulted. But **all such regulations** made by the Governor shall be **laid before each House of the State Legislature for at least 14 days.** The **State Legislature** **can amend or repeal them. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India:
I. 	 The Supreme Court possesses original
and exclusive jurisdiction concerning the enforcement of Fundamental Rights.
II. 	 The writ jurisdiction of the Supreme Court
is absolute and immune from legislative or executive restrictions.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Supreme Court of India**, under Articles **124 to 147**, is the **guardian of the Constitution and the protector** **of Fundamental Rights.** Its jurisdiction is extensive — covering **original**, **appellate**, **advisory**, and **writ powers.**
● The Supreme Court has **original jurisdiction** under **Article 32** to issue writs for the enforcement of **Fundamental** **Rights.**  However, this jurisdiction is **not exclusive,** since **High Courts** also possess concurrent writ jurisdiction under **Article 226.** While the Supreme Court’s jurisdiction is confined **only to Fundamental Rights,** the High Courts can issue writs both for Fundamental Rights and for other legal rights. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **writ jurisdiction** of the Supreme Court, though fundamental, is not absolute.  It can be **restricted, modified,** **or suspended** under certain constitutional provisions, particularly **Article 359 (during National** **Emergency).**  Parliament may also confer upon the Supreme Court additional writ powers “for other purposes,” but cannot make it immune from **constitutional checks.** **So, statement II is not correct.**
o Article 32(3) allows Parliament to confer power to issue directions, orders, or writs for "any purposes other than those mentioned in clause (2) of Article 32" on any other court, but it cannot curtail the Supreme Court's fundamental writ powers for enforcing fundamental rights **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about the
difference between Municipal Corporation and Municipality:
I. 	 Unlike the Mayor of a municipal corporation,
the Chairperson of a municipality enjoys executive powers.
II. 	 The Municipal Commissioner serves as
the Chief Executive Officer of a Municipal Corporation.
III. 	Both are established under the Acts enacted
by the State Legislature.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Municipal corporations** are created for the administration of big cities like Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Hyderabad, Bangalore and others. They are **established in the States by the Acts of the concerned State Legislatures** and in the **Union Territories by the acts of the Parliament of India**. There may be one common Act for all the municipal corporations in a State or a separate Act for each municipal corporation.
● The **municipalities** are established for the administration of **towns and smaller cities.** Like the corporations, they are also set up in the **States by the Acts of the concerned State Legislatures** and in the Union Territories by the Acts of the Parliament of India. **So, statement III is correct.**
● A **municipal corporation** has three authorities, namely, the council, the standing committees and the commissioner. The **council is headed by a Mayor.** He is assisted by a Deputy Mayor. He is elected in a majority of the states for a one-year renewable term.
● Like a municipal corporation, a municipality also has three authorities, namely, the council, the standing committees and the chief executive officer. The **council is headed by a President/Chairman.** He is assisted by a VicePresident/Vice-Chairman. He presides over the meetings of the council. **Unlike the Mayor of a municipal** **corporation**, he plays a significant role and is the pivot of the municipal administration. **Apart from presiding** **over the meetings of the Council, he enjoys executive powers. So, statement I is correct.**
● The **District Magistrate (DM)** is **not the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of a municipal corporation or a** **municipality.** The Municipal Commissioner serves as the CEO of a municipal corporation, while the DM is responsible for overseeing the overall administration of the district, which may encompass multiple municipalities. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Legislation Key Provision
I. - Charter Act of 1853
Introduction of local representation in the Indian Legislative Council for the first time
II. - Indian Councils Act of 1861
Inclusion of Indians in the legislative process for the first time
III. - Indian Councils Act of 1909
Introduction of direct elections for the first time
IV. - Government of India Act of 1919
Women allowed to contest elections for the first time
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Local representation** was first introduced into the **Indian (Central) Legislative Council** by the **Charter Act of** **1853.** This Act established the legislative council with six new members and specified that four of them would be appointed by the **local governments of Madras, Bombay, Bengal, and Agra** to provide local representation. **So,** **pair I is correctly matched.**
o **The Charter Act of 1853** separated, for the first time, the legislative and executive functions of the Governor-General’s council. o It introduced an open competition system of selection and recruitment of civil servants
● The **Indian Councils Act of 1861,** for the first time, associated **Indians** with the **law-making process** by allowing the Viceroy to nominate some **non-official Indian members** to his expanded legislative council. **So, pair II is** **correctly matched.**
o It **initiated the process of decentralisation** by restoring the legislative powers to the Bombay and Madras Presidencies. o It gave **recognition to the ‘portfolio’ system**, introduced by Lord Canning in 1859. o It **empowered the Viceroy to issue ordinances** without the concurrence of the legislative council during an emergency.
● **Direct elections** to the **central legislature** in British India were introduced by the **Government of India Act of** **1919,** which was also known as the **Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms.** Thus, the Indian legislative council was
replaced by a bicameral legislature consisting of an Upper House (Council of State) and a Lower House (Legislative Assembly). The majority of members of both the Houses were chosen by direct election. **So, pair III is not** **correctly matched.**
o **The Indian Councils Act of 1909** provided **(for the first time) for the association of Indians with the** **executive councils** of the Viceroy and Governors. o The Act of 1909 also introduced a **system of communal representation** for muslims by accepting the concept of ‘separate electorate’.
● The **Government of India Act, 1919,** provided for **limited voting rights to women.** Only women with **certain** **income criteria** could be enfranchised by provinces if they wished to do so. However, women were **not allowed** **to contest elections.** **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.**
o **The Government of India Act, 1935** expanded the electorate and explicitly **allowed women to contest** **elections.** After independence, the Constitution of India (1950) introduced **universal adult suffrage,** granting all citizens, including women, the right to vote and contest elections. Today, **the Representation** **of the People Act, 1951** governs the electoral process. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part VIII & X: Union Territories and Scheduled Areas
questions.questionText:
Who among the following is empowered to
organise and reorganise the Autonomous Districts under the Sixth Schedule of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Constitution, under Sixth Schedule, contains special provisions for the administration of tribal areas in the four northeastern states of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram.
● **Key Features of Administration under the Sixth Schedule**
o The tribal areas in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram are designated as autonomous districts. However, they remain under the overall executive authority of their respective state governments. o **The Governor is empowered to organise and reorganise** autonomous districts. This includes altering their boundaries, increasing or decreasing their area, changing their names, or making any other necessary adjustments. o If multiple tribes reside within an autonomous district, the Governor may divide it into separate autonomous regions. o Each autonomous district has a **District Council** with 30 members—26 elected (five-year term unless dissolved earlier) and 4 nominated by the Governor (hold office during the Governor’s pleasure). Autonomous regions also have their own o District and Regional Councils can legislate on matters such as land, forests, shifting cultivation, canal water, village administration, inheritance, marriage and divorce, and social customs. All such laws require the **Governor’s assent**. o Councils may establish village councils or courts to try cases involving tribal members. Appeals lie with the District or Regional Councils. The extent of High Court jurisdiction over these matters is determined by the Governor. o District Councils can set up and manage primary schools, dispensaries, markets, ferries, fisheries, and roads. They can also regulate moneylending and trade by non-tribals—subject to the **Governor’s assent**. o They may assess and collect land revenue, and impose specific taxes within their jurisdiction. o Acts of Parliament or state legislatures do not automatically apply to autonomous districts and regions. They may apply **with exceptions or modifications**, as specified. o The Governor may appoint a commission to review any administrative matter relating to autonomous districts or regions. On its recommendation, he may even **dissolve a District or Regional Council**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Martial Law in India:
I. 	 The expression ‘martial law’ has been defined
in Article 34 of the Constitution of India.
II. 	 An Act of indemnity enacted by Parliament
in connection with martial law cannot be challenged in any court on the ground that it violates the Fundamental Rights.
III. 	The proclamation of martial law does not
suspend the writ of habeas corpus.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **concept of Martial Law** in India is governed by **Article 34** of the Constitution, which provides for **restrictions** **on Fundamental Rights** during its operation. It **authorises Parliament** to indemnify actions taken under martial law and to validate acts done for restoring order. However, unlike the **National Emergency (Article 352),** it is **not** **a codified power** but an implied one, derived from English common law traditions.
● The expression martial law  is **not defined anywhere in the Constitution.**  Article 34 **merely recognises its** **existence** and effects but does not define its scope or procedure for proclamation.  Literally, it means “military rule” — a situation where **civil administration is replaced by military authority,** which assumes extraordinary powers to restore order. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Under Article 34, **Parliament is empowered to indemnify** any government servant or individual for acts done to restore order while martial law is in force.  Parliament may also **validate sentences or punishments** imposed by military authorities during this period.  Such an Act of Indemnity **cannot be challenged in any court** on the ground that it **contravenes Fundamental Rights,** ensuring complete protection to actions taken under martial law. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The Supreme Court has clarified that the **declaration of martial** **law does not** automatically suspend the writ of **habeas corpus** or any **other judicial remedy.** Martial law affects only the **operation of Fundamental Rights** in the areas where it is imposed; courts continue to function, though with restricted jurisdiction. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 All-India Federation
II. 	 Supreme Court at Calcutta
III. 	System of separate budgets in provincial
legislatures
IV. 	 Reserve Bank of India
V. 	 Joint Public Service Commission
How many of the above were provided under the Government of India Act, 1935?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Government of India Act of 1935** represented a pivotal development in the constitutional and political trajectory of colonial India, establishing a foundational framework that facilitated the country's gradual progression toward self-governance. Key Provisions of the Act include:
o It provided for the **establishment of an All-India Federation** consisting of provinces and princely states as units. The Act divided the powers between the Centre and units in terms of three lists–**Federal List** (for Centre, with 59 items), **Provincial List** (for provinces, with 54 items) and the **Concurrent List** (for both, with 36 items). Residuary powers were given to the Viceroy. However, the federation never came into being as the princely states did not join it. **So, point I is correct.** o It **abolished dyarchy in the provinces** and introduced **‘provincial autonomy’** in its place. Moreover, the Act introduced responsible Governments in provinces. o It provided for the **adoption of dyarchy at the Centre.** Consequently, the federal subjects were divided into reserved subjects and transferred subjects. o It introduced **bicameralism in six out of eleven provinces** (Bengal, Bombay, Madras, Bihar, Assam and the United Provinces). o It further extended the **separate electorates** for depressed classes (Scheduled Castes), women and labour (workers). o It **abolished the Council of India,** established by the Government of India Act of 1858. The Secretary of State for India was provided with a team of advisors. o It **extended the franchise.** About **10 per cent of the total population** got the voting right. o The Act established a **Federal Court (in 1937), not a Supreme Court**, and it was set up in **Delhi, not** **Calcutta.** The Supreme Court of India was created in 1950 under the Constitution of Independent India. **So, point II is not correct.**
▪ The **Regulating Act of 1773** provided for the establishment of a **Supreme Court at Calcutta** **(1774)** comprising one chief justice and three other judges.
● The **Government of India Act of 1919 separated, for the first time, provincial budgets from the Central** **budget and authorised the provincial legislatures to enact their budgets.**  **So, point III is not correct.**
● The Act provided for the **establishment of the Reserve Bank of India** to control currency and credit of the country. It was set up in 1935. **So, point IV is correct.**
● It provided for the establishment of not only a Federal Public Service Commission, but also a Provincial Public Service Commission and a **Joint Public Service Commission** for two or more provinces. **So, point V is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I.  	 Sucheta Kripalani was a member of the Flag
Presentation Committee, which presented the first Indian Flag before the Constituent Assembly.
II. 	 Ammu Swaminathan was a member of
the Constituent Assembly who played a prominent role in setting up the Women's India Association at Adyar, Madras.
III.  	Sarojini Naidu was appointed to the
Constituent Assembly from Bihar.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constituent Assembly of India** was formed in 1946 to draft the Constitution of free India. It had 389 members at the beginning (later 299 after partition). While many eminent freedom fighters and leaders were members, some well-known personalities were **not part of the Assembly**
● **In 1946, Sucheta Kripalani was elected to the Constituent Assembly** from the United Provinces. She was a member of the Flag Presentation Committee, which presented the first Indian Flag before the Constituent Assembly. **So, statement I is correct.**
o She was a Gandhian who actively participated in debates on fundamental rights and the role of women in independent India. She later became the **first woman Chief Minister of Uttar Pradesh**.
● **Ammu Swaminathan**, a noted freedom fighter, social activist and a **member of the Constituent Assembly**. She represented Madras and contributed to discussions on social and women’s issues. **So, statement II  is correct.**
o In 1917, she worked with Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay, Annie Besant, Muthulakshmi Reddy, and others to **set up the Women's India Association at Adyar, Madras.**
● **Sarojini Naidu,** the “Nightingale of India,” was a towering figure in India’s freedom struggle and the first woman Governor of an Indian state (U.P.). **Naidu was appointed to the Constituent Assembly from Bihar.** She spoke about the importance of adopting a national flag in the Assembly. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements with reference
to negative and positive liberty are correct?
I. 	 Negative liberty refers to freedom from
external restraint, whereas positive liberty involves having the necessary conditions and capacities to act freely.
II. 	 Negative liberty can exist even amidst
significant social and economic inequalities, while positive liberty seeks to remove such barriers to enable meaningful freedom.
III. 	Negative liberty emphasizes non-interference,
whereas positive liberty focuses on selfmastery and collective empowerment.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Negative liberty refers to the **“freedom from”** external control or interference by the State or others. It stresses the **absence of external constraints**, as long as an individual’s actions do not harm others. Positive liberty, on the other hand, is described as **“freedom to”** — i.e., the presence of enabling conditions (education, health, equality) that allow an individual to act and realise their potential. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Positive liberty, therefore, **addresses structural barriers** — it recognises that genuine freedom requires social and economic empowerment. Positive liberty links closely with welfare state ideals and social justice, seeking to transform mere legal equality into **substantive equality of opportunity**. In contrast, negative liberty is **formally** **compatible with inequality** — a person may be legally free yet practically constrained by poverty, illiteracy, or social discrimination. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Positive liberty goes beyond material conditions: it embodies **self-mastery**, moral autonomy, and collective empowerment. Drawing from Rousseau’s idea of the “general will,” freedom involves participating in decisions that govern one’s life — a blend of **individual autonomy and democratic participation**. Positive liberty thus **goes** **beyond non-interference** (negative liberty) to include **self-realisation, ethical self-discipline, and shared** **responsibility** in society. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the National Investigation Agency (NIA):
I. 	 It is a statutory body under the Ministry of
Home Affairs.
II. 	 The President appoints the Director General
of NIA on the recommendation of a selection committee headed by the Union Home Minister.
III. 	It has the power to probe terror attacks in any
part of the country without the permission of the State Government.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The National Investigation Agency (NIA) was established under the **National Investigation Agency Act, 2008**, making it a **statutory body**. It was enacted in the aftermath of the **26/11 Mumbai terror attacks** to strengthen India’s counter-terrorism framework. The agency functions under the **jurisdiction of the Ministry of Home** **Affairs**, giving it national reach and central oversight. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **NIA is headed by a Director-General (DG)**, who is a senior IPS officer. **The appointment is made by the Union** **Government**, through the Appointments Committee of the Cabinet (ACC). The ACC consists of the Prime Minister and the Home Minister of India. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The **objective** of the NIA is to **investigate and prosecute offences** that threaten India s **sovereignty, security,** **integrity, friendly relations with foreign States**, and matters relating to **international treaties**.
● It has a **nationwide jurisdiction** to investigate and prosecute offences that threaten India’s sovereignty, security, integrity, and friendly relations with foreign States.
o Its primary mandate is to investigate **Scheduled Offences**, which include acts listed in the schedule of the Act, such as the **Atomic Energy Act, 1962**, the **Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act, 1967**, and the **Anti-** **Hijacking Act, 1982**. o The Agency can take up cases on the basis of a report received from a **State Government**, if the **Central** **Government** deems it appropriate, or even **suo motu**, without any state reference.
▪ **Agency can investigate without needing separate permission from states when a scheduled** **offence is involved. So, statement III is correct.** o While investigating a scheduled offence, the NIA has the power to probe **connected offences** committed by the accused. State Governments are legally bound to extend full **assistance and cooperation** for investigations of scheduled offences. o For prosecuting offences under certain legislations, including the **UAPA**, the NIA requires sanction from the Central Government. o Upon completion of the investigation, cases are tried in the **NIA Special Court**, ensuring a streamlined process for prosecution. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
The jurisdiction of the National Investigation Agency extends **throughout India** and also applies **extraterritorially** in specific circumstances. It covers:
● **Indian citizens** residing or travelling outside India.
● **Persons in the service of the Government of India**, regardless of their location.
● **Individuals on Indian-registered ships and aircraft**, wherever they may be.
● **Persons committing a Scheduled Offence outside India** that targets Indian citizens or affects India’s national interest.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the 97th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011:
I. 	 It prescribes the maximum number of
Directors in a cooperative society.
II. 	 Elections to co-operative societies are
supervised by the State Election Commission.
III. 	No co-operative society board shall remain
suspended for over six months, except where there is no government interest or guarantee.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **97th Constitutional Amendment Act of 2011** gave constitutional status and protection to co-operative societies.
o It made the right to form co-operative societies a fundamental right (**Article 19**). o It included a new Directive Principle of State Policy on promotion of co-operative societies **(Article 43-B** ). o It added a new **Part IX-B** in the Constitution, which is entitled “The Co-operative Societies” **(Articles 243-** **ZH to 243-ZT**).
● **Part IX-B** of the Constitution contains the following provisions with respect to the cooperative societies:
o The state legislature may make provisions for the incorporation, regulation and winding-up of co-operative societies based on the principles of voluntary formation, democratic member control, member economic participation and autonomous functioning. o **The superintendence, direction** and control of the preparation of electoral rolls and the conduct of elections to a co-operative society shall **vest in such body**, as may be **provided by the state legislature**. **So, statement II is not correct.**
o **The board** shall consist of such **number of directors** as may be provided by the **state legislature.** But, the **maximum number** of directors of a co-operative society **shall not exceed twenty-one. So, statement I is** **correct.** o The **state legislature** shall provide for the **reservation of one seat for the Scheduled Castes** or the **Scheduled Tribes** and **two seats for women** on the board of every co-operative society having members from such a category of persons. o Board elections must be held before the expiry of the current term to ensure a smooth transition. **No board** shall **remain superseded or suspended for more than six months**, **except** in **societies without** **government shareholding, loans, or guarantees**. In case of supersession, the appointed administrator must conduct elections within six months and transfer management to the newly elected board. . **So,** **statement III is correct.** o The **term of office** of elected members of the board and its office bearers shall be **five years from the date** **of election.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Governance and Management of Cooperative Societies:**
● The **State Legislature shall make provisions for co-option of persons** having experience in the field of banking, management, finance or specialisation in any other related field, as members of the board. But, the **number of such co-opted members shall not exceed two (in addition to** **twenty-one directors).**
● Further, the co-opted members shall not have the right to vote in any election of the co-operative society or be eligible to be elected as office bearers of the board.
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Family Courts in India:
I. 	 These are constituted by the High Court in
consultation with the State Government.
II. 	 Their establishment is mandated in towns/
cities with a population over one million.
III. 	Litigants are not entitled to be represented by
a legal practitioner as a matter of right.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Family Courts Act, 1984** was enacted to provide for the establishment of Family Courts with a view to promote conciliation and secure speedy settlement of disputes relating to marriage and family affairs.
● The salient features of the Family Courts Act, 1984, are as follows:
o It provides for the **establishment** of Family Courts by the **State Governments** in consultation with **the** **High Courts. So, statement I is not correct.** o It makes it **obligatory** on the State Governments **to set** up a Family Court in every city or town **with a** **population exceeding one million. So, statement II is correct.** o It enables the State Governments to set up Family Courts in other areas also, if they deem it necessary. o It exclusively provides within the jurisdiction of the Family Courts the matters relating to:
▪ matrimonial relief, including nullity of marriage, judicial separation, divorce, restitution of conjugal rights, or declaration as to the validity of marriage or as to the matrimonial status of any person ▪ the property of the spouses or of either of them; ▪ declaration as to the legitimacy of any person; ▪ guardianship of a person or the custody of any minor; and ▪ maintenance of wife, children and parents. o It makes it **obligatory on the part of the Family Court** to endeavour, in the first instance**, to effect a** **reconciliation or a settlement between the parties** to a family dispute. During this stage, the proceedings will be informal and rigid rules of procedure shall not apply.
o It provides for the association of social welfare agencies, counsellors, etc., during the conciliation stage and also to secure the service of medical and welfare experts. o **It provides that the parties to a dispute before a Family Court shall not be entitled, as of right, to be** **represented by a legal practitioner.** However, the Court may, in the interest of justice, seek the assistance of a legal expert as amicus curiae. **So, statement III is correct.** o It simplifies the rules of evidence and procedure so as to enable a Family Court to deal effectively with a dispute. o It provides for only one right of appeal, which shall lie to the High Court. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the election of the President of India:
I. 	 The value of votes of elected Members of
Legislative Assemblies is the same across States to maintain uniformity in the scale of representation.
II. 	 Nominated Members of Parliament do not
participate in the election.
III. 	The election is held in accordance with the
system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote and voting is by open ballot.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The President is elected not directly by the people but by members of Electoral College consisting of:
o the elected members of both the Houses of Parliament; o the elected members of the legislative assemblies of the states; and o the elected members of the legislative assemblies of the Union Territories of Delhi and Puducherry.
● The Constitution provides that there shall be uniformity in the scale of representation of different states as well as parity between the states as a whole and the Union at the election of the President. Every elected member of the legislative assembly of a state shall have as many votes as there are multiples of one thousand in the quotient obtained by dividing the population of the state by the total number of elected members of the assembly.
o Thus **value of votes of MLAs differs from state to state**. The value of the votes of MLAs from Uttar Pradesh has the highest value**. So, statement I is not correct.**
● **The Electoral College** for the Presidential election consists of **elected members of both Houses of Parliament** (Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha) and **elected members of the State Legislative Assemblies.** **Nominated members** are **not entitled** to vote in this election.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Every elected member of either House of Parliament** shall have **such number of votes** as may be **obtained by** **dividing the total number of votes assigned to members of the legislative assemblies of the states by the total** **number of the elected members of both the Houses of Parliament.**
● The President’s election is held in accordance with the **system of proportional representation by means of the** **single transferable vote and the voting is by secret ballot.** This system ensures that the successful candidate is returned by the absolute majority of votes. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● A candidate, in order to be declared elected to the office of President, must secure a fixed quota of votes. The quota of votes is determined by dividing the total number of valid votes polled by the number of candidates to be elected (here, only one candidate is to be elected as President) plus one and adding one to the quotient. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
questions.questionText:
A Dilatory Motion in the Parliament of India is
primarily intended to:

options.b: Delay or adjourn the debate on a bill or motion
options.d: Expedite the passage of a bill

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **A Dilatory Motion** in Parliament seeks to adjourn the debate on a bill, motion, or resolution, or **to slow down the** **progress of business in the House.** It can be moved by a member of the Parliament at any time after a motion has been made.
● The debate on this motion is limited to its content, and if the Speaker believes it misuses the House's rules, they may either immediately put the question or reject it.
● A **Censure Motion** can be introduced **only in the Lok Sabha by the Opposition** against the **Council of Ministers**, an **individual Minister**, or a **group of Ministers** for specific acts of omission or commission. It expresses the House’s **disapproval or regret** over the ministers’ actions or policies. The motion must be **specific and self-** **explanatory**, unlike a No-Confidence Motion. If passed, the **Council of Ministers need not resign**, but must **seek** **the confidence of the Lok Sabha** at the earliest.
● An **Adjournment Motion** in Parliament is used to draw attention to a **specific, recent, urgent, and significant** **matter of public importance**. It requires the **support of 50 members** for admission and is considered an **exceptional procedure** as it **suspends normal business** of the House. The debate must last **at least 2 hours and** **30 minutes** and generally **implies censure of the government**, hence it is **not allowed in the Rajya Sabha**. It must concern **one specific issue** and cannot relate to matters of privilege, sub-judice cases, or issues that can be raised through other motions.
● **Calling Attention Motion** can be introduced by a member of Parliament to **bring a matter of urgent public** **importance to the attention of a minister and to request an authoritative statement on the issue**. It is an Indian innovation in parliamentary procedure, introduced in 1954, similar to the Zero Hour. Unlike the Zero Hour, it is specified in the Rules of Procedure. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XV: Elections
questions.questionText:
Which of the following political parties currently
hold the status of a National Party in India?
I. 	 Communist Party of India
II. 	 Aam Aadmi Party
III. 	Bahujan Samaj Party
IV. 	 All India Trinamool Congress
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In India, a political party would be considered (if it satisfies any of the below conditions) a national party if:
o it is ‘recognised’ in four or more states as a state party; or o if its candidates polled at least 6% of total valid votes in any four or more states in the last Lok Sabha or Assembly elections and has at least four MPs in the last Lok Sabha polls; or o if it has won at least 2% of the total seats in the Lok Sabha from not less than three states.
● **National Parties in India:**
o Bhartiya Janata Party o Congress o **Communist Party of India(Marxist).** o **Bahujan Samaj Party (BSP). So, point III is correct.** o National People’s Party (NPP) o **Aam Aadmi Party. So, point II is correct.**
● The Election Commission of India has **revoked** the status of the **All India Trinamool Congress, Nationalist** **Congress Party (NCP)** and **the Communist Party of India (CPI). So, points I and IV are not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
questions.questionText:
Consider the following provisions:
I. 	 Voting age was increased from 18 years to 21
years
II. 	 The Ninth Schedule was added
III. 	The Veto power of the President on the
Constitutional Amendment Bill was removed
IV. 	 Enabled land reform and acquisition for
public welfare
V. 	 Recognized public order, state security, and
foreign relations as valid grounds to restrict the Freedom of Speech under Article 19(1)

options.a:
Of the Constitution of India
How many of the above were added to the Constitution of India by the First Constitutional Amendment Act, 1951?

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The First Constitutional Amendment Act, 1951 did not change the voting age. **Voting age was later set to 21** **years by the Representation of the People Act, 1950, and reduced to 18 years by the Sixty-first Amendment** **in 1988. So, point I is not correct.**
● **Provisions** added by the **First Constitutional Amendment Act, 1951:**
o Land Reform and Right to Property
▪ Amended Articles 19(1)(f) and 31 to empower the State to impose reasonable restrictions on the right to property. ▪ **Enabled land reform** and **acquisition for public welfare**, essential to dismantle the zamindari system. **So, point IV is correct.** o Freedom of Speech and Expression
▪ Revised Article 19(2) to expand the grounds for restrictions on free speech. ▪ Included **public order, security of the state, and foreign relations** as valid reasons for **imposing** **limitations. So, point V is correct.** o Social Justice for SCs and STs
▪ Inserted Articles 15(4) and 16(4) to allow reservations in education and employment for Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and other socially and educationally backward classes. o Validation of Land Reform Laws
▪ Added Article 31A to protect agrarian reform laws from being struck down for violating property rights. ▪ Inserted **Article 31B** and the **Ninth Schedule** to **insulate** such laws from **judicial review. So, point** **II is correct.** o Directive for Upliftment of Weaker Sections
▪ Strengthened Article 46, directing the State to promote the educational and economic interests of SCs, STs, and other weaker sections with special care.
● **The Twenty-fourth Constitutional Amendment Act, 1971 made it mandatory for the President to provide** **assent to a constitutional amendment bill, removing any veto power for the President on such bills. So, point** **III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements about
residuary powers is not correct?

options.a:
Before independence, the residuary powers
under the Government of India Act, 1935, were vested in the Governor-General.
options.b:
Unlike in India, residuary powers in the
United States are vested in the States.
options.c:
The provision of residuary powers in the
Constitution of India was adopted from the Constitution of Canada.
options.d:
The subject of ‘Weights & Measures’ falls
under the residuary powers of the Union as per the Seventh Schedule.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 248 of the Constitution of India** vests the **Union Government with residuary legislative powers**, which refer to the authority to legislate on subjects that are **not enumerated in the Union List, State List, or Concurrent** **List**. These powers ensure that any matter not explicitly mentioned in the Constitution automatically falls under the **exclusive jurisdiction of the Parliament**, thereby enabling the Union to address emerging or unforeseen areas of legislation. Residuary powers thus play a crucial role in maintaining **legislative flexibility and national uniformity** in subjects not contemplated at the time of the Constitution’s enactment.
● Under the  **Government of India Act, 1935** , any **subject not included** in the **Federal, Provincial, or Concurrent** **Lists** fell under the exclusive authority of the **Governor-General**, reflecting a highly centralised colonial administration.
● Under the Constitution of the United States of America, the **Tenth Amendment** provides that powers not explicitly delegated to the federal government are **reserved for the States**, reflecting a greater degree of state autonomy. In contrast, the **Constitution of India vests residuary powers in the Union**, indicating the framers’ intention to establish a **strong Central authority** to maintain national unity and ensure uniformity in areas not specifically enumerated in any list.
● The Constitution of India draws several federal features from Canada, including the provision that **residuary** **powers rest with the Union.**
● **Weights and measures** is **not** a **residuary subject.** It is explicitly mentioned in the **Concurrent List** of the Seventh Schedule. 42nd Amendment Act, 1976, shifted 5 subjects from State List to Concurrent List:
o Education o Forests o **Weights & Measures** o Administration of Justice o Protection of Wild Animals & Birds. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
The **Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India** systematically distributes legislative powers between the Union and the States through **three distinct lists**:
● **Union List (List I):** Comprises subjects of **national importance** where the **Parliament has** **exclusive authority** to legislate. These typically include matters essential for national governance, defence, foreign affairs, and macroeconomic policy.
● **State List (List II):** Encompasses subjects of **local or regional significance**, over which the **state legislatures possess exclusive legislative competence**. This enables states to tailor laws according to regional needs and priorities.
● **Concurrent List (List III):** Contains subjects on which **both Parliament and state legislatures** **may legislate**. In the event of a **conflict between a Union law and a state law** on a concurrent subject, the **Union law prevails**, ensuring national uniformity while accommodating statespecific variations in non-conflicting areas.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Vice-President of India,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 As the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya
Sabha, the Vice-President must meet the same minimum age requirement as a member of the Rajya Sabha.
II. 	 The office of the Vice-President is not
considered an Office of Profit under the Constitution of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Vice-President of India** is the second-highest constitutional office in the country, created under **Article 63 of** **the Constitution.** He functions as the **ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha** and acts as the President when the office of the President falls vacant.
● Under **Article 66(3) of the Constitution**, a person to be eligible for election as Vice-President must:
o be a **citizen of India**, o have completed **35 years of age**, and o be qualified for election as a **member of the Rajya Sabha** (but not necessarily a member).
● **For membership in the Rajya Sabha, the minimum age requirement is 30 years (Article 84(b)).** **So, statement** **I is not correct.**
● Article 66(4) provides that “the **Vice-President shall not hold any other office of profit**”, but being ex officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha does not count as holding such an office. Further, under **Article 102(1)(a),** certain constitutional offices such as the **President, Vice-President, Governors, or Ministers are not considered offices** **of profit. So, statement II is correct.**
● **The Vice-President of India can be removed by a resolution initiated and passed in the Rajya Sabha by an** **effective majority and agreed to by the Lok Sabha by a simple majority, as per Article 67(b).** The resolution can only be introduced in the Rajya Sabha after a 14-day notice. The Constitution specifies no grounds for the VicePresident’s removal. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
With reference to the ordinance-making power of
the Governor, consider the following statements:
I. 	 S/He can promulgate an ordinance when
either of the two Houses of a bicameral State Legislature is not in session.
II. 	 S/He cannot promulgate an ordinance on a
subject listed in the Concurrent List.
III. 	S/He promulgates an ordinance at their own
discretion.
IV.  	An ordinance ceases to operate upon the
expiration of six months from the date of reassembly of the State Legislature.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 213** of the Constitution of India **empowers the Governor of State to promulgate ordinances** during the recess of the State Legislature. He **can promulgate an ordinance only when s/he is satisfied** that certain circumstances exist which render it necessary for him to take immediate action.
● The Governor can promulgate an ordinance only when the legislative assembly (in case of a unicameral legislature) is not in session. The Governor can promulgate an ordinance **when both Houses of a bicameral State Legislature** **are not in session or when either of the two Houses of a bicameral State Legislature is not in session**. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The **Governor’s ordinance-making power is co-extensive with the State Legislature’s legislative authority**. This means that he/she can issue ordinances on subjects that the State Legislature can legislate on. It means that he/she can **promulgate ordinances on the subjects mentioned in the State List as well as the Concurrent List**. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **The ordinance-making power of the Governor is not a discretionary power.** This means that he can promulgate or withdraw an ordinance only on the advice of the State Council of Ministers. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● **The ordinance-making power of the Governor under Article 231 is not immune from judicial review.** The Court is empowered to adjudge whether there is a valid exercise of the power.
● **An ordinance issued by the Governor ceases to operate on the expiry of six weeks (not six months) from the** **reassembly of the State Legislature**. It may cease to operate even earlier than the prescribed six weeks, if a resolution disapproving it is passed by the legislative assembly and is agreed to by the legislative council (in case of a bicameral legislature). **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
Neeti is a District Judge in Jhajjar (Haryana). In
this context, which one of the following statements is correct?

options.a:
She was appointed by the Chief Justice of the
Punjab and Haryana High Court.
options.b:
She had a combined experience of seven years
as an advocate and judicial officer in the State Judicial Service before her appointment.
options.c:
She has the power to impose the death penalty
without confirmation from the High Court.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Articles 233 to 237** in **Part VI** of the **Constitution of India** lay down provisions for regulating the **organisation** **of subordinate courts** and ensuring their **independence from the executive**.
● The **appointment, posting, and promotion** of **district judges** in a state are made by the **Governor of the state** in **consultation with the High Court**. Therefore, Neeti was appointed by the **Governor of Punjab and Haryana.** **So, option (a) is not correct.**
o A district judge **should be recommended by the High Court** for appointment and should have been an advocate or a pleader for at least seven years. Hence, Neeti was **recommended by the High Court** of Punjab and Haryana.
● **A district judge** **should not already be in the service** of the **Central or State Government**. District Judges are considered for appointment to the High Court. So far, only practising lawyers with seven years’ experience were eligible for direct recruitment as District Judges. o In-service judicial officers, even with seven years of previous practice as advocates, were not considered, leading to stagnation and lack of drive, which the court observed may be one of the reasons for pendency in lower courts. o However, in a **Constitution Bench headed by Chief Justice of India B.R. Gavai** held that a person who has been or **who is in judicial service and has a combined experience of seven years** or more as an advocate and a Judicial Officer would be eligible for being considered and appointed as a **District Judge/Additional District Judge under** **Article 233 of the Constitution**. Therefore, Neeti was eligible for the appointment. **So, option (b) is correct.** o The Constitution Bench directed that the minimum age for being considered and appointed as a District Judge or Additional District Judge, both for advocates and judicial officers, would be **35 years** as on the date of application.
● The **District Judge** is the **highest judicial authority in the district** and possesses both **original and appellate** **jurisdiction** in **civil** as well as **criminal matters**. In other words, the **District Judge** also functions as the **Sessions** **Judge**. When he/she deals with **civil cases**, he/she is referred to as the **District Judge**, and when he/she hears **criminal cases**, he/she is called the **Sessions Judge**. o The **session judge** has the **power to impose any sentence**, including **life imprisonment** and **capital punishment** **(death sentence)**. However, any **capital punishment** awarded by the Sessions Judge is **subject to confirmation** **by the High Court**, **whether or not an appeal is filed**. Hence, Neeti does not have the power to impose the death penalty without confirmation from the High Court.  **So, option (c) is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About the Subordinate Courts:**
● The **District Judge** exercises both **judicial and administrative powers** and also holds **supervisory authority** over all **subordinate courts** within the district. **Appeals** against the **orders and judgments** of the District Judge lie with the **High Court**.
● The **Sessions Judge**, who is the same person when handling criminal cases.
● Below the **District and Sessions Court**, there exist lower courts on both the **civil** and **criminal** sides: o On the **civil side** stands the **Court of Subordinate Judges**, who exercise **unlimited pecuniary** **jurisdiction** over civil suits. o On the **criminal side**, the **Court of the Chief Judicial Magistrate** decides **criminal cases** **punishable with imprisonment up to seven years**.
● At the **lowest level**:
o On the **civil side** is the **Court of Munsiff**, which has **limited jurisdiction** and deals with **civil cases of small pecuniary value**. o On the **criminal side** is the **Court of Judicial Magistrate**, which tries **criminal cases** **punishable with imprisonment up to three years**.
● In **metropolitan cities**, there are **City Civil Courts (Chief Judges)** for civil matters and **Courts** **of Metropolitan Magistrates** for criminal cases.
● In some **states and presidency towns**, **Small Causes Courts** have been established to decide **civil cases of small value** in a **summary manner**. Their decisions are **final**, but the **High Court** retains the **power of revision**.
● In several **states**, **Panchayat Courts** exist to handle **petty civil and criminal cases**. They are variously known as **Nyaya Panchayat**, **Gram Kutchery**, **Adalati Panchayat**, or **Panchayat** **Adalat**, and serve as instruments of **local-level justice delivery**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the national emergency:
I. 	 Applying a national emergency beyond
disturbed areas lacks constitutional justification, given its impact on Fundamental Rights in peaceful regions.
II. 	 The satisfaction of the President in proclaiming
the emergency cannot be questioned in any court of law.
III. 	The emergency can be continued for an
indefinite period of time, provided the Union Cabinet approves it after every six months.
IV. 	 The Rajya Sabha has no role with respect to a
resolution disapproving of the emergency.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 352** of the Constitution of India **empowers the President of India to declare a National Emergency** when the security of India or a part of it is threatened by war or external aggression or armed rebellion.
o The President of India **can proclaim a national emergency only after receiving a written** **recommendation from the Union Cabinet.**
● A proclamation of national emergency **may be applicable to the entire country** or only a part of it. **Even if the** **disturbances are restricted to certain regions of the country, the national emergency may be applicable to** **the entire country.** **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **38th Amendment Act of 1975 made the declaration of a National Emergency immune from judicial** **review.** It provided that the satisfaction of the President in the imposition of a National Emergency shall be final and conclusive and shall not be questioned in any court on any ground. **However, this provision was subsequently** **deleted by the 44th Amendment Act of 1978**. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The proclamation of National Emergency must be approved by both the Houses of Parliament within one month from the date of its issue. If approved by both the Houses of Parliament, the **emergency continues for six months,** **and can be extended to an indefinite period with the approval of Parliament for every six months.** This provision for periodical parliamentary approval was also added by the 44th Amendment Act of 1978. **So, statement** **III is not correct.**
o Thus, **the approval of Parliament is required, not just executive.**
● A proclamation of emergency **may be revoked by the President** at any time by a subsequent proclamation. Such a proclamation does not require parliamentary approval. Also, the President must revoke a proclamation **if the Lok** **Sabha passes a resolution disapproving its continuation.** **Rajya Sabha will have no role with respect to a** **resolution disapproving the emergency. So, statement IV is correct.**
o A resolution of disapproval is different from a resolution approving the continuation of a proclamation in the following two respects:
▪ The first one is required to be passed by the Lok Sabha only, while the second one needs to be passed by the both Houses of Parliament. ▪ The first one is to be adopted by a simple majority only, while the second one needs to be adopted by a special majority. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Foundations & Framework
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard
to the Supreme Court of India and the Supreme Court of the United States:
I. 	 Both possess the power to grant special leave
to appeal in any matter.
II. 	 Unlike the Supreme Court of the United
States, the Supreme Court of India exercises supervisory authority over the High Courts.
III. 	The Supreme Court of India relies on
‘procedure established by law’, whereas the Supreme Court of the United States follows ‘due process of law’.
IV. 	 The scope of judicial review in India is
broader than that in the United States.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Supreme Court of India** enjoys a **wide discretionary power** to grant **special leave to appeal** in any case against the judgment of any court or tribunal (except military tribunals). The **U.S. Supreme Court**, however, has **no such plenary power**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Unlike the American Constitution, the Constitution of India establishes an integrated judicial system, with the Supreme Court at the apex and the High Courts subordinate to it. Beneath the High Courts lies a hierarchy of
subordinate courts, including District and other lower courts.
