This unified structure—derived from the Government of India Act of 1935—administers both Central and State laws. o In contrast, the United States follows a dual judicial system, wherein federal laws are enforced by the federal judiciary and state laws by the respective state judiciaries. **Consequently, while the Supreme Court of India** **exercises superintendence and control over the High Courts, the American Supreme Court lacks such** **authority under its federal framework. Hence, statement II is correct.**
● The **Supreme Court of India’s scope** of judicial review is **limited**, ensuring that laws follow the **“procedure** **established by law”**, while the **Supreme Court of United States** exercises a **very wide scope of judicial review** based on the **“due process of law”** doctrine. **So, statement III is correct and statement IV is not correct.**
● The **Supreme Court of India** also possesses an **advisory jurisdiction**, allowing the **President of India** to seek its opinion on matters of public importance under **Article 143**, while the **Supreme Court of United States** has **no** **advisory jurisdiction**, as it only decides actual disputes.
● The **jurisdiction and powers** of the **Indian Supreme Court** can be **enlarged by Parliament**, whereas those of the **American Supreme Court** are **strictly confined to what is conferred by the Constitution**.
● The **original jurisdiction** of the **Supreme Court** of India is confined to **federal cases**, such as disputes between the Centre and the states, while the **Supreme Court of United State’s original jurisdiction** covers not only **federal** **cases** but also matters relating to **naval forces, maritime activities, and ambassadors**.
● In **appellate jurisdiction**, the **Supreme Court** of India deals with **constitutional, civil, and criminal cases**, whereas the **Supreme Court of United State’s appellate jurisdiction** is **limited to constitutional cases only**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.questionText:
Why is the Comptroller and Auditor General of
India (CAG) of India **not** a comptroller in practice?

options.a:
The CAG cannot sanction the demands for
grants of ministries before they are presented in the budget.
options.b:
The CAG has no control over the issue of
money from the Consolidated Fund of India.
options.c:
The CAG performs only advisory functions
related to public expenditure.
options.d:
The CAG’s role is limited to maintaining
accounts of public sector undertakings.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Constitution of India (Article 148)** provides for an independent office of the **Comptroller and Auditor** **General of India (CAG).** She/he is the head of the **Indian Audit and Accounts Department**. She/he is the guardian of the public purse and controls the entire financial system of the country at both the levels–the Centre and the State. The CAG is **appointed by the President of India** by a warrant under his hand and seal.
● The Constitution of India envisages the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) as both a **Comptroller** and an **Auditor General**. However, in practice, the **CAG performs only the role of an Auditor General,** not that of a Comptroller. This means the **CAG does not control the withdrawal of funds from the Consolidated Fund**; various departments can issue **cheques and draw money without the CAG’s prior approval.** The CAG’s involvement begins only at the audit stage, after the expenditure has occurred. In contrast, the British CAG exercises both comptroller and auditor functions and the executive there can withdraw money from the public exchequer only with the CAG’s approval. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A recent CAG performance audit revealed systemic flaws in the GST e-way bill system, raising concerns about the accuracy of tax reporting.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XVIII: Emergency Provisions
questions.questionText:
Which one of the statements given below is
correct with regard to the National Emergency proclaimed on the grounds of external aggression?

options.a:
The legislative powers of the State Legislatures
stand suspended during the operation of the Emergency.
options.b:
The tenure of Parliament may be extended,
but this provision does not apply to the State Legislatures.
options.c:
The President can approve a gender-based
division of labour by suspending the right to move any court for the enforcement of Article 15.
options.d:
The Parliament can delegate the power to
make laws for the States to the President.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 352** of the Constitution of India **empowers the President of India to declare a National Emergency** when the security of India or a part of it is threatened by war or external aggression, or armed rebellion.
o When a national emergency is declared **on the grounds of ‘war’ or ‘external aggression’**, it is known as ‘**External Emergency’.** On the other hand, when it is declared **on the ground of ‘armed rebellion’**, it is known as **‘Internal Emergency’.**
● During a national emergency, the Parliament becomes empowered to make laws on any subject mentioned in the State List. **However, the legislative power of a State Legislature is not suspended. So, option (a) is not correct.**
● During a National Emergency, the **Parliament may extend the life of the Lok Sabha** beyond its normal term for one year at a time (for any length of time). However, this extension cannot continue beyond a period of six months after the emergency has ceased to operate.
o **Similarly, the Parliament may extend the normal tenure of a state legislative assembly by one year at** **a time**. **So, option (b) is not correct.**
● Articles 358 and 359 describe the effect of a National Emergency on the Fundamental Rights of the Citizens. **Article** **359 authorises the President to suspend the right to move any court for the enforcement of Fundamental** **Rights during a National Emergency, including Article 15 (Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of** **religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth) of the Constitution.** However, the President cannot suspend the right to move the Court for the enforcement of fundamental rights guaranteed by Articles 20 to 21. In other words, the right to protection in respect of conviction for offences (Article 20) and the **right to life and personal liberty** **(Article 21) remain enforceable even during an emergency. So, option (c) is correct.**
● The emergency suspends the distribution of legislative powers between the Union and the States. When a national emergency is proclaimed, the Parliament becomes empowered to make laws on any subject mentioned in the State List. **The Parliament cannot delegate this power to the President.** **So, option (d) is not correct.**
o **During the President’s rule, the Parliament can delegate the power to make laws for the State to the** **President or to any other authority specified by him.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Chief Minister, State Council of Ministers
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Chief Minister of a State in
India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 A person who is not a member of the State
Legislature cannot be appointed as the Chief Minister.
II. 	 Her/his resignation or death automatically
dissolves the Council of Ministers.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 164** of the Constitution provides that the **Chief Minister shall be appointed by the Governor** and the other Ministers shall be appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Chief Minister.
● It further says that “a Minister who for any period of six consecutive months is not a member of the Legislature of the State shall at the expiration of that period cease to be a Minister.” Thus, **a person who is not a member of the** **state legislature can be appointed as Chief Minister for six months**, within which time, he should be elected to the state legislature; otherwise, he ceases to be the Chief Minister. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **The Chief Minister directs, controls and coordinates the activities of the Council of Ministers.** Since the Chief Minister is the head of the Council of Ministers, **his resignation or death automatically dissolves the Council of** **Ministers**. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
questions.questionText:
With reference to the provisions of the 73rd
Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Gram Sabha has been made a constitutional
body.
II. 	 A three-tier government system is mandated
in all the States.
III. 	A panchayat reconstituted after premature
dissolution does not enjoy the full period of five years.
IV. 	 It lays down that no election to any panchayat
is to be questioned except by an election petition presented to the High Court.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **73rd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992** added a new **Part-IX** to the Constitution of India. This part is entitled ‘The Panchayats’ and consists of provisions from **Articles 243 to 243-O.** The Act has also added a new **Eleventh Schedule** to the Constitution. **The Act provides a constitutional status to the panchayati raj** **institutions.**
● **The act provides for a Gram Sabha as the foundation of the panchayati raj system.  The Act provides a** **constitutional status to it.** It is a body consisting of persons registered in the electoral rolls of a village within the area of the Panchayat at the village level. Thus, it is a village assembly consisting of all the registered voters in the area of a panchayat. It may exercise such powers and perform such functions at the village level as the legislature of a state determines**. So, statement I is correct.**
● **The Act provides for a three-tier system of panchayati raj in every state, that is, panchayats at the village,** **intermediate, and district levels.** Thus, the act brings about uniformity in the structure of panchayati raj throughout the country. **However, a state having a population not exceeding 20 lakh may not constitute panchayats at the** **intermediate level. So, statement II is not correct.**
● The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992 provides for a five-year term of office to the panchayat at every level. However, it can be dissolved before the completion of its term. Fresh elections to constitute a panchayat shall be completed before the expiry of a period of six months from the date of its dissolution.
o **A new panchayat reconstituted after premature dissolution does not enjoy the full period of five years** **but remains in office only for the remainder of the period.** If the remainder of the period is less than six months, then it is not necessary to hold any election to constitute the new panchayat for such a period. **So,** **statement III is correct.**
● The Act bars interference by courts in the electoral matters of panchayats. It declares that the validity of any law relating to the delimitation of constituencies or the allotment of seats to such constituencies cannot be questioned in any court. It further lays down that **no election to any panchayat is to be questioned except by an election**
**petition presented to such authority and in such manner as provided by the State Legislature**. **So, statement** **IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
questions.questionText:
At the Central level, which one of the following
Ministries deals with the subject of ‘Urban Local Government’?

options.c: Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The system of urban government was constitutionalised through the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1992. At the Central level, the subject of ‘urban local government’ is dealt with by the following three ministries:
o Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs. o **Ministry of Defence in the case of the cantonment boards** o Ministry of Home Affairs in the case of Union Territories
● “Local government” is a subject under the State List of the Seventh Schedule of the Indian Constitution. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
questions.questionText:
Which of the following powers is/are conferred
on the State Legislature by the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992?
I. 	 The State Legislature may provide for the
representation in Municipalities for members of the Rajya Sabha registered as electors within the municipal area.
II. 	 The State Legislature may provide for the
reservation of seats for women for the post of Chairpersons of Municipalities.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **74th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992,** gave **constitutional status to the municipalities**. It has added a new **Part IX-A** to the Constitution of India. This part is entitled ‘The Municipalities’ and consists of provisions from **Articles 243-P to 243-ZG.** The Act has also added a new **Twelfth Schedule** to the Constitution. This schedule contains eighteen functional items of municipalities.
● **Article 243R** of the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992 says that all the seats in a Municipality shall be filled by persons chosen by direct election. It further says that the **State Legislature may provide for the** **representation of the following in a Municipality:**
o persons having special knowledge or experience in Municipal administration; o the members of the Lok Sabha and the members of the Legislative Assembly of the State representing constituencies which wholly or partly comprise the Municipal area; o **the members of the Rajya Sabha** and the members of the Legislative Council of the State registered as electors within the Municipal area; **So, statement I is correct.**
● Article 243T of the Act provides for the reservation of seats for the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes in every municipality. It also provides for the reservation of not less than one-third of the total number of seats for women.
o It further says that the **offices of Chairpersons in the Municipalities shall be reserved for the Scheduled** **Castes, the Scheduled Tribes, and women** in such manner as the Legislature of a State may, by law, provide. **So, statement II is correct.** o It also empowers the State Legislature to make any provision for the reservation of seats in any municipality or offices of chairpersons in municipalities in favour of backward classes. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Governor of a State in India,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 Unlike the President, ministerial advice has
not been made binding on the Governor by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment, 1976.
II. 	 Any dispute regarding whether a matter falls
within the Governor’s discretion or not is decided by the Constitutional Bench of the Supreme Court.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Article 74(1) provides that there shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at the head, to aid and advise the President in the exercise of his functions. Although the President may require the Council of Ministers to reconsider such advice, but the President shall act in accordance with the advice tendered after such reconsideration. Thus, **the President is constitutionally bound to act on the aid and advice of the Union Council** **of Ministers.**
● The Governor is the Executive Head of the State.  **Article 163 provides that there shall be a Council of Ministers** **with the Chief Minister at the head to aid and advise the Governor in the exercise of his functions, except in** **so far as he is by or under this Constitution required to exercise his functions or any of them at his discretion.**
● From the above, it is clear that the constitutional position of the governor differs from that of the president in the following two respects
o While the Constitution envisages the possibility of the governor acting at times in his discretion, no such possibility has been envisaged for the President. o **After the 42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976), ministerial advice has been made binding on the** **President, but no such provision has been made with respect to the Governor.** **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● If any question arises regarding whether a matter falls within the Governor’s discretion or not, **the decision of the** **Governor is final.** Also, the validity of anything done by the Governor shall not be called in question on the ground that he ought or ought not to have acted in his discretion.  **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XV: Elections
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct
with regard to the procedure for the election to the State Legislative Assemblies in India?
I. 	 The minimum age for contesting an election
and for voting in the elections is the same.
II. 	 Only the Parliament is empowered to make
laws regarding the elections to the State Assemblies.
III. 	The Supreme Court has exclusive original
jurisdiction to decide election petitions relating to the Assembly elections.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● According to Article 173 (b) of the Constitution, the **minimum age for becoming a candidate** for Vidhan Sabha (Legislative Assembly) election **is twenty-five years**. While, as per Article 326 of the Constitution,  **the minimum** **age for registration as a voter is 18 years**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Article 327 empowers the Parliament to make provision with respect to elections to State Legislatures. It says that “subject to the provisions of this Constitution, **Parliament may make provision with respect to all matters** **relating to elections to either House of Parliament or to the House or either House of the Legislature of a** **State**.”
o But **Article 328 also authorises the State Legislature** **to make provision with respect to elections to** **such Legislature.** It says that “subject to the provisions of this Constitution and in so far as provisions in that behalf is not made by Parliament, the Legislature of a State may make provision with respect to all matters relating to the elections to the House of the Legislature of the State”. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● **High Courts have original jurisdiction** on disputes relating to the election of members of Parliament and state legislatures. Election petitions are filed in the High Court of the particular state in which the election was conducted. **Petitioners have a right to appeal in the Supreme Court against the High Court’s order. So, statement III is** **not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Chief Minister, State Council of Ministers
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the State Council of Ministers:
I. 	 As per the Constitution of India number of
ministers, including the Chief Minister in a State, shall not be less than twelve.
II. 	 The advice tendered by the Council of
Ministers to the Governor can be questioned in a court of law on the ground of being arbitrary or malicious.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 164** of the Constitution of India provides that “the total number of Ministers, including the Chief Minister, in the Council of Ministers in a State **shall not exceed fifteen per cent of the total number of members of the** **Legislative Assembly of that State**”.
o It also says that the **number of Ministers, including the Chief Minister in a State, shall not be less than** **twelve.**  **So, statement I is correct.**
● Article 163 says that the **advice tendered by Ministers to the Governor shall not be inquired into in any court**. **So, statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI):
I. 	 It is an attached office under the Ministry of
Personnel, Public Grievances, and Pensions.
II. 	 It draws its powers from the Delhi Special
Police Establishment Act, 1946.
III. 	The tenure of the Director of CBI, including
extensions, shall not exceed five years
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) is India's premier investigative agency. Additionally, it serves as the nodal agency coordinating investigations for Interpol member countries.
● The CBI is neither a constitutional nor a statutory body. **It functions under the Ministry of Personnel, Public** **Grievances, and Pensions as an attached office** and plays a vital role in upholding law and order by investigating complex cases of national interest**. So, statement I is correct.**
● In 1963, the CBI was officially formed following a resolution from the Ministry of Home Affairs, based on the recommendations of the Santhanam Committee on Prevention of Corruption. It was subsequently transferred to the Ministry of Personnel, Public Grievances, and Pensions, enhancing its operational framework.
● It **derives its legal authority and investigative powers** from the provisions of the **Delhi Special Police** **Establishment (DSPE) Act, 1946. So, statement II is correct.**
● The CBI is led by a Director, typically an Indian Police Service (IPS) officer of the rank of Director General of Police.
● **The Director of the Central Bureau of Investigation is appointed by the Appointments Committee of the** **Cabinet,** based on the **recommendations of a Selection Committee, as stipulated in the Delhi Special Police** **Establishment (DSPE) Act of 1946**. The Appointment Committee is composed of:
o Prime Minister – Chairperson o Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha or the Leader of the single largest opposition party in the Lok Sabha – Member o Chief Justice of India or a Supreme Court Judge recommended by the Chief Justice – Member o If there is no recognised LoP, then the leader of the single largest party is included in the committee
● The President promulgated two ordinances enabling the central government to extend the tenures of the Directors of the **Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI)** and the **Enforcement Directorate (ED)** from the existing two-year term up to a maximum of five years.
o Amendments to the **Delhi Special Police Establishment (DSPE) Act, 1946** and the **Central** **Vigilance Commission (CVC) Act, 2003** now allow the government to extend the tenure of these officials by one year at a time, based on the recommendation of the Committee comprising the **Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition, and the Chief Justice of India**, with reasons recorded in writing. However, the **total tenure**, including the initial term and all extensions, **cannot exceed five years**. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The CBI functions under the overall superintendence of the Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) in matters arising under the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
● The CVC also chairs the committee responsible for the selection of the Director of the CBI, as well as officers of the rank of Superintendent of Police and above in the DSPE, the investigative wing of the CBI.
● The CBI primarily investigates corruption cases, economic offenses, and conventional crimes. Its anti-corruption efforts focus on offenses committed by employees of the Central Government, Union Territories, and public sector undertakings. For serious crimes like murder, kidnapping, and rape, the CBI takes up cases upon referral from state governments or when directed by the Supreme Court or High Courts.
● It is exempted from the Right to Information Act. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
